_note: CAET Advanced practice bank. 193 items 1:1 to real exam stems, paraphrased + position-shuffled + length-balanced.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the core function of an FMS.
questions.fullObjective: Explain that the flight management system's primary role is automating navigation and performance calculations for the flight crew.
questions.targetSourceStem: What primary function does the flight management system provide to the flight crew?

flashcard.definition: Automates navigation computations and aircraft performance calculations so the crew can manage the flight plan rather than hand-fly each leg.
flashcard.analogy: Like a car's GPS combined with a fuel-economy computer working together for the pilots.

jeopardy.question: This is the main service an FMS delivers to the flight crew during normal operations.
choices: Ground proximity and traffic collision alerting
Automatic engine start and FADEC sequencing
Direct manual reversion of flight controls
jeopardy.explanation: The FMS was designed to offload navigation and performance workload from the crew; alerting, engine starts, and manual reversion belong to other dedicated systems.

multipleChoice.question: Which core service does the flight management system deliver to the pilots of a transport-category aircraft?
choices: Automation of navigation and performance calculations
Alerting for conflicting traffic and nearby terrain
Automated sequencing of engine start procedures
Direct manual reversion of primary flight controls
multipleChoice.explanation: An FMS integrates sensor data to manage the flight plan and compute performance; TCAS, TAWS, FADEC, and flight controls are separate systems.

practice.question: Scenario: A new-hire technician asks why the flight crew relies on the FMS for most of the cruise segment. Which answer best describes the FMS's primary contribution?
choices: It performs the navigation and performance calculations automatically.
It issues alerts when nearby traffic or terrain becomes a threat.
It sequences the engine start valves during taxi operations.
It provides mechanical reversion when the autopilot is disengaged.
practice.explanation: The FMS continuously computes position, route tracking, fuel burn, and vertical path automation, which is its defining purpose in the cockpit.

preassessment.question: From the cockpit's standpoint, what is the core capability that an FMS delivers to the pilots?
choices: Conflict alerts for nearby traffic and terrain
Automated engine start and FADEC control
Manual reversion of the flight control system

questions.learningObjective: Identify the sensors that supply position data to the FMC.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the FMC blends GPS, inertial reference, air data, and VOR/DME inputs to compute an accurate aircraft position.
questions.targetSourceStem: Which combination of systems provides position data to the flight management computer?

flashcard.definition: The FMC fuses GPS, IRS, ADC, and VOR/DME inputs to compute the current aircraft position.
flashcard.analogy: Like a phone using GPS, Wi-Fi, and cell towers together to pin down its location when one signal is weak.

jeopardy.question: These four sensor sources are blended by the FMC to produce a fused aircraft position.
choices: Pitot probe and radar altimeter pair
Weather radar feed and transponder reply
jeopardy.explanation: An FMC fuses satellite, inertial, air data, and ground-based radio navigation sources; the other options do not produce a navigational position solution.

multipleChoice.question: Which set of onboard sensors supplies position inputs to the flight management computer?
choices: A standalone magnetometer plus the TCAS processor
GPS receivers, IRS, ADC, and VOR/DME radios
The weather radar feed and transponder output
A pitot probe paired with the radar altimeter
multipleChoice.explanation: FMC position fusion uses GPS for absolute fixes, IRS for continuity, ADC for altitude and airspeed, and VOR/DME for ground-referenced updates.

practice.question: Scenario: While tracing FMC position inputs on a schematic, which combination of sensors should the technician expect to find wired to the computer?
choices: A magnetometer plus the TCAS processor
GPS, IRS, ADC, and VOR/DME inputs
Weather radar output and the transponder
A pitot tube and radar altimeter feed
practice.explanation: The position solution requires satellite, inertial, air data, and radio-based sources; the distractors provide surveillance or single-axis data, not a position fix.

preassessment.question: Which sensor combination feeds position data to the FMC?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the major components in a typical FMS installation.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a typical FMS installation consists of a CDU, FMC, navigation database, and supporting sensor inputs.
questions.targetSourceStem: What components make up a typical flight management system installation?

flashcard.definition: A standard FMS installation is built from a CDU for crew entry, an FMC for computation, a navigation database, and connected sensor LRUs.
flashcard.analogy: Like a desktop computer: the keyboard, CPU, hard drive, and peripherals each play a specific role.

jeopardy.question: This set of LRUs forms the architecture of a conventional FMS installation.
choices: AHRS, ADC, and audio control panel units
FDR, CVR, and ELT recorder bundle
CDU, FMC, navigation database, and sensors
MFD, VHF radio, and TAWS processor
jeopardy.explanation: A complete FMS is defined by its CDU interface, FMC computation engine, navigation database storage, and sensor inputs; the other groupings describe recording, attitude/air-data, or display/comm systems.

multipleChoice.question: Which group of line-replaceable units defines a typical FMS installation?
choices: Multifunction display, VHF communication radio, and TAWS processor
Flight data recorder, cockpit voice recorder, and emergency locator transmitter
Control display unit, flight management computer, navigation database, and sensors
Attitude heading reference, air data computer, and audio control panel
multipleChoice.explanation: The CDU, FMC, nav database, and sensor suite together form an FMS; the other answers describe recorders, attitude or comm systems.

practice.question: Scenario: A technician is mapping the FMS architecture on a new aircraft. Which major components should appear in the block diagram of a typical FMS?
choices: FDR, CVR, and ELT as primary FMS elements
AHRS, ADC, and audio panel as the FMS core
CDU, FMC, navigation database, and sensor inputs
MFD, VHF radio, and TAWS as FMS modules
practice.explanation: The FMS block diagram always centers on the FMC, with a CDU, a nav database, and sensor LRUs feeding it.

preassessment.question: Which components are found in a typical FMS installation?
preassessment.explanation: A typical FMS installation consists of the CDU (control display unit), FMC (flight management computer), navigation database, and position sensors.

questions.learningObjective: Identify where published procedures are stored for the FMS.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that published STAR and SID procedures are held in the FMS navigation database, not elsewhere in the aircraft.
questions.targetSourceStem: The pilot wants to fly a published STAR procedure. Where is this procedure data stored?

flashcard.definition: Standard Terminal Arrival Routes are stored in the FMS navigation database, which is updated on the AIRAC 28-day cycle.
flashcard.analogy: Like address books on your phone: the routes live in the device's stored database, not on an outside server.

jeopardy.question: This is where a published STAR procedure physically lives inside the avionics suite.
jeopardy.explanation: Published procedures such as STARs and SIDs are held in the FMS nav database; the distractors are ground systems, aircraft-identity storage, or maintenance tools.

multipleChoice.question: When a pilot selects a published STAR on the CDU, which onboard storage location holds the procedure data?
choices: The navigation database loaded in the FMS
multipleChoice.explanation: All coded approaches, SIDs, and STARs reside in the FMS nav database and are refreshed every 28 days.

practice.question: Scenario: A pilot reports she cannot load yesterday's newly published STAR. Where should the technician look first to confirm the procedure is available onboard?
choices: The ADS-B ground station database timestamp
The FMS navigation database currency and version
practice.explanation: Procedure availability depends entirely on whether the procedure has been published in the currently loaded FMS nav database.

preassessment.question: Where is a published STAR procedure stored onboard the aircraft?
choices: In an ADS-B ground station database

questions.learningObjective: Identify the interface parameter verified during FMS installation.
questions.fullObjective: Verify that ARINC 429 label assignments between LRUs are correctly mapped during FMS installation.
questions.targetSourceStem: During FMS installation, what interface parameter must be verified for proper system communication?

flashcard.definition: ARINC 429 label mapping between each LRU must be verified so that the FMS sends and receives the correct parameters on the correct buses.
flashcard.analogy: Like checking that every mailing address in a company matches the right department before sending internal memos.

jeopardy.question: This is the interface parameter that technicians must verify during FMS installation to ensure LRUs talk correctly.
jeopardy.explanation: FMS communication depends on ARINC 429 labels being routed and received correctly across each connected LRU; the distractors are unrelated cabin or comm adjustments.

multipleChoice.question: During FMS installation, which interface parameter must the technician verify between LRUs?
multipleChoice.explanation: Label mapping determines which data each LRU transmits and receives; errors here cause missing or misinterpreted FMS data.

practice.question: Scenario: The shop is integrating a new FMS into a King Air. Before sign-off, what interface parameter must the technician confirm for system communication?
choices: Correct ARINC 429 label mapping to each LRU
Correct ADF loop antenna orientation angle
Correct cabin reading light dimmer position
practice.explanation: Without correct ARINC 429 label routing, sensors and displays will not exchange FMS data correctly, regardless of other configuration.

preassessment.question: What interface parameter is verified during FMS installation?
choices: ARINC 429 label mapping to each LRU

questions.learningObjective: Identify the data bus standard connecting the FMC and cockpit displays.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize ARINC 429 as the prevailing digital data bus used between the FMC and cockpit displays.
questions.targetSourceStem: What data bus standard is used for communication between the FMC and cockpit displays?

flashcard.definition: ARINC 429 is the one-way digital data bus standard used to carry FMC data to cockpit displays.
flashcard.analogy: Like a one-way radio broadcast that displays can tune in to without talking back.

jeopardy.question: This digital data bus standard carries FMC output to cockpit display units.
jeopardy.explanation: ARINC 429 is the civil aviation standard for one-way data; RS-232, MIL-STD-1553, and CAN bus are used elsewhere but not for FMC-to-EFIS communication.

multipleChoice.question: Which data bus standard is typically used to carry data from the FMC to cockpit displays?
multipleChoice.explanation: ARINC 429 dominates civil transport FMC interfaces; MIL-STD-1553 is military, RS-232 is slow short-range serial, and CAN is used in engine and utility systems.

practice.question: Scenario: The technician is tracing signal paths from the FMC to the primary flight displays. Which data bus standard should be used to decode those signals?
practice.explanation: Decoding FMC-to-display signals requires ARINC 429 tools, matching the standard used by the installation.

preassessment.question: Which data bus standard connects the FMC to cockpit displays?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the crew interface device for FMS data entry.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize the CDU keyboard as the primary device the crew uses to enter FMS flight plan data.
questions.targetSourceStem: What device does the pilot use to enter waypoints and flight plan data into the FMS?

flashcard.definition: The Control Display Unit (CDU) provides the keyboard pilots use to enter waypoints, flight plans, and route modifications into the FMS.
flashcard.analogy: Like the keyboard and screen on a shared office computer where users type their requests.

jeopardy.question: This is the device pilots use to type waypoints and flight plan data into the FMS.
choices: The EFIS range and mode controller
jeopardy.explanation: The CDU keyboard is dedicated to FMS data entry; the other controls manage audio, transponder codes, or display range.

multipleChoice.question: Which cockpit device is used by the pilot to enter waypoints into the FMS?
choices: The EFIS range and mode controller
multipleChoice.explanation: The CDU is the FMS user interface; the other devices control audio routing, transponder squawk, or display formatting.

practice.question: Scenario: A pilot needs to manually enter a fresh waypoint mid-flight. Which cockpit device accepts that entry into the FMS?
choices: The EFIS range and mode controller
The audio control panel source selector
The CDU keyboard and line-select keys
practice.explanation: Waypoint entry is performed on the CDU keyboard; none of the distractor devices can write to the FMS database.

preassessment.question: Where does the pilot enter FMS waypoints?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the coupling capability verified during FMS-autopilot integration.
questions.fullObjective: Verify that the FMS provides both lateral and vertical guidance coupling to the autopilot during integration testing.
questions.targetSourceStem: When integrating FMS with the autopilot, what capability must the technician verify?

flashcard.definition: Both lateral (LNAV) and vertical (VNAV) guidance signals from the FMS must couple correctly to the autopilot during integration checkout.
flashcard.analogy: Like confirming a driving instructor can steer and brake, not just one of the two.

jeopardy.question: This is the coupling capability a technician must confirm when tying the FMS into the autopilot.
choices: Weather radar overlay on the display
jeopardy.explanation: Coupling is checked in both the LNAV and VNAV axes; autobrakes, radar overlays, and ADS-B are not autopilot coupling features.

multipleChoice.question: Which autopilot capability must be verified during FMS integration testing?
choices: Weather radar overlay routing to displays
multipleChoice.explanation: An FMS is only useful as an automation source if both LNAV and VNAV commands drive the autopilot correctly.

practice.question: Scenario: A shop is completing an FMS-to-autopilot integration. Which capability must be specifically verified on the post-installation checkout?
choices: Weather radar imagery routing to the MFD
Arming and disarm logic for the autobrake system
ADS-B In and Out transmit/receive function
Lateral and vertical guidance coupling from FMS to autopilot
practice.explanation: The core integration requirement is that FMS steering and vertical path commands drive the autopilot; the distractors are separate system checks.

preassessment.question: What must the technician verify when integrating FMS with autopilot?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the test equipment used to verify ARINC 429 output from the FMC.
questions.fullObjective: Select a dedicated ARINC 429 data bus analyzer to confirm digital output from the flight management computer.
questions.targetSourceStem: What test equipment verifies ARINC 429 data transmission from the flight management computer?

flashcard.definition: A dedicated data bus protocol analyzer captures and decodes ARINC 429 labels transmitted by the FMC.
flashcard.analogy: Like using a software packet sniffer to read Wi-Fi traffic instead of a simple voltmeter.

jeopardy.question: This is the test tool used to decode ARINC 429 data from an FMC.
choices: A dedicated data bus protocol analyzer
A digital multimeter with frequency counter
A fiber optic power level meter
jeopardy.explanation: Only a protocol analyzer decodes ARINC 429 labels and words; DMMs, pitot-static sets, and optical meters cannot interpret the bus traffic.

multipleChoice.question: Which piece of test equipment verifies ARINC 429 transmissions from the FMC?
choices: A dedicated data bus protocol analyzer
A digital multimeter with frequency counter
A fiber optic power level meter
multipleChoice.explanation: A bus analyzer is required to capture labels, parity, and SSM bits; the distractors measure voltage, pneumatics, or light intensity.

practice.question: Scenario: A technician needs to verify that the FMC is transmitting correct ARINC 429 labels. Which test tool should be selected?
choices: A digital multimeter with frequency mode
A fiber optic power level meter
practice.explanation: Verifying labels and data validity requires a protocol analyzer; other instruments cannot parse ARINC 429 words.

preassessment.question: Which tool verifies ARINC 429 data from the FMC?

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose why an autopilot fails to capture an LNAV route from the FMS.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a missing or mislabeled ARINC 429 roll steering signal is the most likely cause when LNAV fails to track after autopilot engagement.
questions.targetSourceStem: A pilot reports the aircraft will not intercept the programmed LNAV route after engaging the autopilot. The CDU shows the correct flight plan. What should the technician check?

flashcard.definition: If the flight plan is correct but the autopilot will not track LNAV, the ARINC 429 roll steering output from the FMC to the autopilot is the first suspect.
flashcard.analogy: Like having the right destination in the GPS but the steering wheel unplugged from the driver.

jeopardy.question: This is the FMC output a technician should inspect when LNAV is programmed correctly but the autopilot refuses to capture the route.
choices: ARINC 429 roll steering output to the autopilot
Audio panel transmit and receive routing
jeopardy.explanation: Roll steering (also called roll command) is the FMC's lateral command to the autopilot; without it, the autopilot cannot track the programmed route.

multipleChoice.question: An autopilot fails to intercept the programmed LNAV route though the CDU shows the correct flight plan. Which signal path should be inspected first?
choices: The ARINC 429 roll steering command to the autopilot
The VOR receiver's signal strength output
The audio panel's transmit and receive routing
multipleChoice.explanation: Lateral capture relies on the roll steering word; all other systems listed are unrelated to LNAV routing.

practice.question: Scenario: After takeoff, the autopilot engages but the aircraft does not capture the programmed LNAV course. The CDU flight plan is verified correct. Where should the technician troubleshoot first?
choices: The VOR receiver's signal strength indication
The ARINC 429 roll steering word from FMC to autopilot
The pitot-static system leak test records
The audio panel transmit and receive routing
practice.explanation: Roll steering is the lateral command link; if it is missing or corrupt, LNAV displays correctly but the autopilot cannot follow it.

preassessment.question: The autopilot won't track LNAV though the flight plan is correct. What should be checked?
choices: ARINC 429 roll steering to the autopilot

questions.learningObjective: Identify the test that confirms valid GPS position data into the FMS.
questions.fullObjective: Perform a sensor data validity and alignment test to verify the FMS is receiving valid GPS position data.
questions.targetSourceStem: What test confirms the FMS is receiving valid GPS position data?

flashcard.definition: A sensor data validity and alignment test compares the FMS-displayed position against the GPS position to confirm valid input.
flashcard.analogy: Like checking that two clocks show the same time before trusting either for an appointment.

jeopardy.question: This is the test that proves the FMS is accepting valid GPS position data.
choices: A VHF standing wave ratio check
A sensor data validity and alignment test
A DC bus voltage under load test
jeopardy.explanation: Verifying position validity requires comparing sensor inputs against the FMS solution; the distractors test unrelated audio, antenna, or power parameters.

multipleChoice.question: Which test confirms that valid GPS position data is reaching the flight management system?
choices: A DC bus voltage loaded test
A sensor data validity and alignment test
A VHF antenna standing wave ratio check
multipleChoice.explanation: A sensor alignment and validity test shows whether the FMS is accepting and aligning the GPS position correctly.

practice.question: Scenario: Before releasing an aircraft after a GPS antenna swap, which test should the technician perform to confirm the FMS is seeing valid GPS data?
choices: Perform a VHF standing wave ratio check
Measure the audio panel sidetone level
Run the sensor data validity and alignment test
Load-test the DC bus voltage stability
practice.explanation: The sensor validity test verifies both signal presence and that the FMS accepts the GPS position; the other tests evaluate different systems.

preassessment.question: To verify that the FMS has acquired usable GPS information, which bench procedure should the technician run?
choices: Sensor data validity and alignment test
DC bus voltage under load test

questions.learningObjective: Explain what happens to the active flight plan when a direct-to clearance is entered.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that entering a direct-to modifies the active leg so the FMS routes the aircraft directly to the selected waypoint.
questions.targetSourceStem: The pilot enters a direct-to clearance into the CDU. What change occurs in the active flight plan?

flashcard.definition: A direct-to entry shortens the active flight plan so the active leg terminates at the selected waypoint, cutting out intermediate legs.
flashcard.analogy: Like telling a GPS 'just get me to the hotel' and it drops the scenic route.

jeopardy.question: This change occurs in the active flight plan when a direct-to clearance is entered on the CDU.
choices: The autopilot disconnects for manual flight
VNAV is disabled for the remainder of the flight
The FMS drops into dead reckoning mode
The active leg shifts to direct to the selected waypoint
jeopardy.explanation: Direct-to rewrites the active leg; it does not disable VNAV, disconnect the autopilot, or force dead reckoning.

multipleChoice.question: After the pilot commands a direct-to to a downstream waypoint, how does the active flight plan change?
choices: The FMS switches to dead reckoning for navigation
All vertical navigation modes are disabled
The autopilot disconnects and manual flight is required
The active leg is rewritten to go direct to the waypoint
multipleChoice.explanation: Direct-to edits the lateral flight plan; VNAV stays available, the autopilot remains engaged, and GPS/IRS still drive position.

practice.question: Scenario: ATC clears the aircraft direct to FRONK. The pilot enters the direct-to on the CDU. What change should the technician expect on the displayed flight plan?
choices: The FMS reverts to dead reckoning mode
The active leg changes to go direct to FRONK
practice.explanation: The FMS simply retargets the active leg; autopilot, VNAV, and sensor modes remain unaffected.

preassessment.question: A direct-to entry changes the flight plan how?
preassessment.explanation: A direct-to entry truncates the active leg so it proceeds directly to the selected waypoint.

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose an LNAV tracking failure after a new FMS installation.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that incorrect roll steering wiring or ARINC 429 label assignment is the most likely installation error when vertical modes work but lateral tracking fails.
questions.targetSourceStem: After installing a new FMS, the autopilot will not track the programmed lateral route. Vertical modes work normally. What is the most likely installation error?

flashcard.definition: When VNAV works but LNAV does not after installation, suspect roll steering wiring or incorrect ARINC 429 label assignments on the lateral channel.
flashcard.analogy: Like a car where the brake pedal works but the steering column was never bolted in.

jeopardy.question: This installation error best explains why LNAV is failing while VNAV works normally on a newly installed FMS.
choices: Roll steering wiring or label assignment error
Vertical gyro power supply connection issue
GPS antenna coaxial cable routing error
jeopardy.explanation: Since VNAV is working, altitude, air data, and GPS position must be healthy; only the lateral roll steering path can cause the described split.

multipleChoice.question: After a new FMS install, vertical modes function normally but the autopilot will not track LNAV. Which installation error is most likely?
choices: Roll steering wiring or ARINC label error
Pitot-static plumbing error to the ADC
multipleChoice.explanation: Healthy VNAV means air data and GPS are working; the fault must be isolated to the lateral steering path (roll steering wiring or ARINC 429 label assignment) between the FMC and the autopilot.

practice.question: Scenario: Post-installation testing shows VNAV captures and holds correctly, but the autopilot will not track lateral routes. Where should the technician focus troubleshooting?
choices: The roll steering wiring and ARINC 429 label mapping
The vertical gyro power supply connections
The pitot-static plumbing to the ADC
The GPS antenna coaxial cable routing
practice.explanation: LNAV isolation to lateral steering indicates the roll command path, not air-data, gyro power, or GPS antenna.

preassessment.question: VNAV works, LNAV doesn't, after a new FMS install. Likely cause?
preassessment.explanation: Healthy VNAV means air data and GPS are working; the lateral fault is isolated to roll-steering wiring or ARINC 429 label assignment.

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose why a SID does not appear in the FMS procedure list.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that an outdated or incomplete navigation database is the usual reason a published SID is missing from the FMS.
questions.targetSourceStem: The pilot attempts to load a published SID but the procedure does not appear in the FMS. The airport identifier is correct. What is the cause?

flashcard.definition: If the airport identifier is correct but the SID is unavailable, the navigation database is probably outdated or the regional coverage is incomplete.
flashcard.analogy: Like searching for a new restaurant that opened after your map app was last updated.

jeopardy.question: This is the usual reason a correctly identified airport offers no selectable SID in the FMS.
choices: The GPS receiver has insufficient satellite coverage
The navigation database is outdated or incomplete
The CDU display brightness is set too low
The FMS software is stuck in maintenance mode
jeopardy.explanation: Procedure availability depends on the currency and coverage of the nav database; GPS, brightness, and maintenance mode would produce different symptoms.

multipleChoice.question: A pilot cannot find a published SID in the FMS even though the airport code is correct. What is the most likely cause?
choices: CDU display brightness is too dim to see
An outdated or incomplete navigation database
Insufficient GPS satellite geometry or coverage
The FMS software is still in maintenance mode
multipleChoice.explanation: Missing procedures are a database problem; GPS and display brightness issues cause different failure symptoms.

practice.question: Scenario: A pilot reports a brand-new SID is missing from the FMS though the airport ID is correct. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The GPS has poor satellite coverage at that airport
The nav database is outdated or does not include the region
The CDU brightness is set too low to see the list
The FMS is stuck in maintenance mode
practice.explanation: If the FMS does not show the SID, the most common reason is a nav database that does not contain the procedure.

preassessment.question: A SID doesn't appear in FMS though airport is correct. Cause?

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose an FMC position invalid fault on power-up.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that GPS and IRS inputs must be verified when the CDU displays an FMC position invalid message at power-up.
questions.targetSourceStem: One CDU displays 'FMC POSITION INVALID' during power-up. What inputs should the technician verify?

flashcard.definition: When the CDU annunciates FMC POSITION INVALID, start by verifying the GPS and IRS signals reaching the FMC.
flashcard.analogy: Like your phone showing no map fix until you confirm both Wi-Fi and GPS are actually turned on.

jeopardy.question: These are the first inputs to verify when an FMC POSITION INVALID alert appears on the CDU at power-up.
choices: Replace the CDU with a known good unit
GPS and IRS data reaching the FMC
Audio panel RF emissions from the headset bus
jeopardy.explanation: Position validity depends on sensor inputs; swapping CDUs, checking audio RF, or testing fuel wiring would not address an FMC position issue.

multipleChoice.question: A CDU displays 'FMC POSITION INVALID' at power-up. Which inputs should be verified first?
choices: Audio panel RF emissions onto the comm bus
Swap the CDU for a known-good spare
The GPS and IRS signals arriving at the FMC
multipleChoice.explanation: Position invalid messages are triggered by missing or unhealthy position sensors; verify GPS and IRS first.

practice.question: Scenario: At power-up, one CDU reads 'FMC POSITION INVALID' while the other CDU is normal. Which inputs should the technician check on the failing side?
choices: Replace the CDU with a known-good unit
The GPS and IRS data inputs into that FMC
practice.explanation: The message indicates the FMC has no valid position source; checking GPS and IRS inputs addresses the root cause.

preassessment.question: CDU shows FMC POSITION INVALID. What to verify?
choices: GPS and IRS inputs to the FMC

questions.learningObjective: Explain why stored flight plans are lost during a battery replacement.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the FMS memory lost its backup power during the battery change, clearing stored flight plans.
questions.targetSourceStem: After battery replacement, all saved flight plans in the FMS are lost. What caused the data loss?

flashcard.term: FMS data loss at battery swap
flashcard.definition: Stored flight plans disappear when the FMS memory loses power during a battery change because some units rely on backup power to retain volatile memory.
flashcard.analogy: Like unplugging a computer that hasn't saved your document yet.

jeopardy.question: This condition during a battery replacement explains why stored FMS flight plans vanish.
choices: Fuel quantity computer lost its calibration
VHF NAV radio was left in self-test mode
FMS memory lost backup power during the swap
jeopardy.explanation: Volatile memory in the FMS depends on continuous power or a backup supply; the distractors have no connection to stored route data.

multipleChoice.question: Following an aircraft battery change, all saved FMS flight plans are gone. What caused the loss?
choices: The VHF NAV receiver was left in self-test mode
The fuel quantity computer lost calibration
The FMS volatile memory lost power during the swap
multipleChoice.explanation: Without standby power, volatile memory in many FMCs clears stored plans; other listed items are unrelated to route storage.

practice.question: Scenario: After a battery replacement, the pilot reports all stored company flight plans are gone from the FMS. What is the cause?
choices: VHF NAV was left in self-test mode
The fuel computer lost calibration data
FMS memory lost power during the battery swap
practice.explanation: Volatile FMS memory requires continuous power; bypassing the battery without a ground power connection can clear stored plans.

preassessment.question: Why would FMS stored plans be lost after battery change?
choices: VHF NAV was in self-test mode
FMS memory lost power during swap

questions.learningObjective: Identify the inputs to check when VNAV descent profiles are inaccurate.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that ADC pressure data and aircraft performance data are the primary inputs affecting VNAV descent accuracy on a STAR.
questions.targetSourceStem: The pilot reports inaccurate VNAV descent profiles after loading a STAR. What system inputs should be checked?

flashcard.definition: VNAV descents rely on ADC pressure and performance data; inaccurate descents warrant checking air data inputs and the performance initialization.
flashcard.analogy: Like a cruise control that can't keep a steady speed if the tire pressure sensor is lying.

jeopardy.question: These system inputs should be verified when VNAV descent profiles are inaccurate after loading a STAR.
choices: ADC pressure data and performance initialization
jeopardy.explanation: Accurate VNAV requires correct air data and aircraft performance inputs; the distractors have no role in descent computation.

multipleChoice.question: A pilot reports the aircraft is flying inaccurate VNAV descents on STARs. Which inputs should be checked?
choices: ADC pressure data and FMS performance data
multipleChoice.explanation: VNAV accuracy depends on pressure altitude and performance model data; the other systems have no influence on descent computations.

practice.question: Scenario: After multiple STAR descents, the pilot reports the FMS is consistently early or late on crossing restrictions. Which inputs should the technician inspect?
choices: ADC pressure data and FMS perf init
practice.explanation: Descent path prediction uses air data pressures plus FMS performance initialization; errors in either source produce inaccurate crossing profiles. The distractors affect unrelated systems.

preassessment.question: Inaccurate VNAV descent profiles on STAR. Check?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the FMS mode that computes vertical profiles to altitude restrictions.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the vertical navigation mode computes the optimum profile from cruise to a crossing altitude.
questions.targetSourceStem: Which FMS mode computes the optimum vertical path from cruise altitude to a crossing restriction?

flashcard.definition: Vertical navigation (VNAV) calculates the idle-optimum descent path from cruise altitude to a published crossing restriction.
flashcard.analogy: Like planning a bike descent so you coast and arrive at the bottom at a set speed.

jeopardy.question: When the flight crew wants an automatic top-of-descent calculation to meet a crossing altitude, they engage this FMS mode.
jeopardy.explanation: Vertical path optimization is the VNAV mode's defining role; LNAV handles lateral path, direct-to modifies routing, and holding manages loops.

multipleChoice.question: When the FMS calculates an idealized top-of-descent profile to make a published crossing altitude, what navigation function is being used?
multipleChoice.explanation: VNAV produces the descent profile; LNAV manages lateral course, direct-to shortcuts routing, and holding manages race-track patterns.

practice.question: Scenario: A pilot asks the technician which FMS mode is responsible for computing the idle descent path to a crossing altitude on a STAR. Which mode is correct?
choices: The holding pattern mode of the FMS
The VNAV mode of the FMS
The direct-to mode of the FMS
The LNAV mode of the FMS
practice.explanation: VNAV is the dedicated vertical path planner in the FMS; other modes manage horizontal routing or holding.

preassessment.question: Which FMS mode computes the vertical profile to a crossing restriction?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the compass error caused by ferrous airframe materials.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that magnetic deviation is the error caused by ferrous metals and aircraft electrical systems affecting the magnetic compass.
questions.targetSourceStem: Ferrous metals in the aircraft structure cause the magnetic compass to read differently than actual magnetic heading. What is this error called?

flashcard.definition: Deviation is the compass error induced by ferrous aircraft structure and installed electrical systems, corrected during compass swing.
flashcard.analogy: Like a kitchen compass that points wrong because of a refrigerator magnet nearby.

jeopardy.question: This compass error is introduced by ferrous metals and electrical systems in the aircraft itself.
jeopardy.explanation: Deviation is aircraft-induced; variation is geographic, dip is latitude-based, and precession is a gyro property.

multipleChoice.question: What is the name of the magnetic compass error produced by ferrous aircraft structure and electrical systems?
multipleChoice.explanation: Deviation is corrected by the compensator screws during a compass swing; the others describe geography, latitude, or gyroscopic effects.

practice.question: Scenario: A pilot asks why the compass reads slightly off on certain headings after avionics were added. How should the technician label this aircraft-induced error?
choices: Magnetic precession of the compass card
Magnetic variation between true and magnetic north
Magnetic dip caused by Earth's inclination
practice.explanation: Deviation is the correct term for aircraft-induced compass error; the other terms describe different phenomena.

preassessment.question: What is the compass error from ferrous aircraft structure called?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the angular difference between true and magnetic north.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that magnetic variation (declination) is the geographic angle between true north and magnetic north at a given location.
questions.targetSourceStem: During compass swing, the technician must account for the angular difference between true north and magnetic north at the aircraft location. What is this difference called?

flashcard.definition: Variation, also called declination, is the geographic angle between true north and magnetic north, changing with location and time.
flashcard.analogy: Like the gap between 'where the map says north is' and 'where the planet's magnet actually points.'

jeopardy.question: This is the geographic angular difference between true north and magnetic north at a given location.
jeopardy.explanation: Variation is purely a location-based angle; deviation is aircraft-caused, dip is a vertical effect, and precession is a gyro property.

multipleChoice.question: What is the angular difference between true north and magnetic north at a given point on Earth called?
choices: Gyroscopic precession of a spinning rotor
multipleChoice.explanation: Variation is plotted on aeronautical charts as isogonic lines and must be applied when converting between true and magnetic headings.

practice.question: Scenario: During compass swing, the technician notes the aircraft's location has a 7 degree east value on the chart. What is this 7 degree angle called?
practice.explanation: Chart-annotated angles between true and magnetic north are variation, not aircraft or gyro errors.

preassessment.question: The angle between true and magnetic north is called?
preassessment.explanation: Magnetic variation, also called declination, is the angle between true north and magnetic north at a given location.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the adjustment devices used during compass swing.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that compass compensator adjustment screws are the small magnets used to null out deviation during a compass swing.
questions.targetSourceStem: After a compass swing, the technician adjusts small magnets to minimize the difference between compass reading and known heading. What are these devices?

flashcard.definition: Compass compensator adjustment screws hold small magnets that the technician turns to cancel aircraft-induced deviation during a compass swing.
flashcard.analogy: Like turning tuning knobs on a guitar until each string matches a reference pitch.

jeopardy.question: These small magnets are adjusted during compass swing to null out heading errors.
jeopardy.explanation: The compensator screws are the adjustment mechanism on the compass itself; the distractors belong to flux gates, directional gyros, and slaving systems.

multipleChoice.question: Which devices does a technician adjust during a compass swing to reduce heading error?
choices: The flux gate detector sensing coils
multipleChoice.explanation: Compass swings use small magnets (compensator screws) built into the compass; the distractors are elements of other heading systems.

practice.question: Scenario: While performing a compass swing, the technician uses a non-ferrous screwdriver to adjust small magnets inside the compass housing. What are these devices called?
practice.explanation: Compensator screws are the adjustment mechanism inside a magnetic compass; none of the distractors are located inside the compass.

preassessment.question: The small magnets adjusted during compass swing are called?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the gyroscopic property that stabilizes a spinning rotor.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize gyroscopic rigidity as the property by which a spinning rotor resists changes to its orientation.
questions.targetSourceStem: A directional gyro maintains its heading reference because the spinning rotor resists changes to its orientation. What is this property called?

flashcard.definition: Rigidity in space is the gyroscopic property by which a spinning rotor resists changes to its axis orientation.
flashcard.analogy: Like a spinning bike wheel that refuses to tip over once it's up to speed.

jeopardy.question: This gyroscopic property lets a spinning rotor hold its orientation against applied forces.
jeopardy.explanation: Rigidity keeps the spin axis fixed in space; precession, nutation, and torque describe how the rotor responds to forces, not how it resists them.

multipleChoice.question: Which gyroscopic property allows a directional gyro's rotor to resist changes in orientation?
multipleChoice.explanation: Rigidity is the resistance to motion; precession is the 90-degree force response, nutation is oscillation, and torque is the applied force itself.

practice.question: Scenario: A trainee asks why a spinning directional gyro stays pointed in the same direction even as the aircraft maneuvers. Which property explains this?
practice.explanation: Rigidity is the property by which the rotor resists changes, which is exactly what the trainee is observing.

preassessment.question: What property keeps a gyro rotor pointing the same direction?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the causes of directional gyro drift.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize apparent precession and bearing friction as the primary causes of directional gyro drift over time.
questions.targetSourceStem: A vacuum-driven directional gyro gradually drifts from the correct heading over time. What causes this drift?

flashcard.definition: Directional gyros drift because Earth rotation produces apparent precession and bearing friction adds real precession.
flashcard.analogy: Like a top that slowly tilts because the table it sits on is rotating under it.

jeopardy.question: These two effects combine to make a vacuum-driven DG drift off heading over time.
jeopardy.explanation: Earth rotation and bearing friction are the classic drift causes; vacuum loss stops the gyro entirely, and RF or heat are not normal drift sources.

multipleChoice.question: What causes a vacuum-driven directional gyro to drift slowly from correct heading during flight?
choices: A steady loss of vacuum pump pressure
multipleChoice.explanation: Drift stems from Earth rotation plus mechanical friction; vacuum failure would cause gyro tumble, not slow drift.

practice.question: Scenario: A pilot reports the DG reading needs resetting against the compass about every 15 minutes. What is the cause of this drift?
choices: Gradual loss of vacuum pump pressure in flight
Apparent precession and bearing friction in the gyro
practice.explanation: Regular slow drift is characteristic of apparent precession plus friction, which is why DGs must be periodically reset to the magnetic compass.

preassessment.question: Why does a vacuum DG drift over time?
preassessment.explanation: A vacuum directional gyro drifts because of apparent precession (earth rotation) and bearing friction in the gimbals.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the reference used to correct the DG periodically.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize the magnetic compass as the reference used to correct the directional gyro for drift during flight.
questions.targetSourceStem: The directional gyro reading is compared to another instrument and corrected periodically. What instrument provides the reference?

flashcard.definition: The magnetic compass serves as the heading reference used to periodically correct a directional gyro for drift.
flashcard.analogy: Like syncing a kitchen timer back to the wall clock every hour.

jeopardy.question: This instrument is used as the reference to periodically correct the DG for drift.
jeopardy.explanation: Only the magnetic compass provides an absolute heading reference; the others display different parameters.

multipleChoice.question: Which cockpit instrument provides the reference for periodic correction of a directional gyro?
multipleChoice.explanation: Only the magnetic compass measures heading; VSI indicates climb/descent, AI shows pitch/roll, and turn coordinator shows rate of turn.

practice.question: Scenario: Every 15 minutes the pilot realigns the DG to match another instrument during straight and level flight. Which instrument is used?
practice.explanation: The compass provides the only absolute heading reference in a mechanical setup, so it is used to correct DG drift.

preassessment.question: What instrument references DG heading corrections?

questions.learningObjective: Expand the AHRS acronym.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize AHRS as the Attitude Heading Reference System used in modern avionics.
questions.targetSourceStem: What does the acronym AHRS represent in modern avionics systems?

flashcard.definition: AHRS stands for Attitude Heading Reference System and replaces mechanical vertical and directional gyros with solid-state sensors.
flashcard.analogy: Like swapping a wristwatch with gears for a smartwatch with chips.

jeopardy.question: These words are the expansion of the acronym AHRS in avionics.
jeopardy.explanation: AHRS always expands to Attitude Heading Reference System; the other choices are fabricated plausible-sounding terms.

multipleChoice.question: What does the acronym AHRS stand for in modern avionics?
multipleChoice.explanation: AHRS is the industry-standard expansion and appears in all civil and military avionics documentation.

practice.question: Scenario: A new technician asks what AHRS stands for when labeling a wiring diagram. Which answer is correct?
practice.explanation: Only Attitude Heading Reference System is the correct expansion of AHRS; the other choices are distractor acronyms.

preassessment.question: AHRS stands for what?

questions.learningObjective: Explain how MEMS gyros sense rotation.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that MEMS gyroscopes detect rotation through the Coriolis force acting on vibrating mass elements.
questions.targetSourceStem: Unlike traditional spinning-mass gyros, MEMS gyroscopes in modern AHRS units detect rotation using a different principle. How do MEMS gyros sense angular rate?

flashcard.definition: MEMS gyroscopes sense angular rate by measuring how vibrating elements deflect under the Coriolis force.
flashcard.analogy: Like feeling a sideways push when you walk straight across a spinning carousel.

jeopardy.question: MEMS gyros sense angular rate through this physical principle instead of using a spinning mass.
choices: Magnetic sensors detecting Earth field rotation
Vibrating elements deflected by the Coriolis force
Laser beams shifting phase in an optical cavity
jeopardy.explanation: MEMS gyros rely on Coriolis deflection of a vibrating structure; laser and spinning rotor principles describe different gyro types, and Earth field is magnetometry.

multipleChoice.question: How do MEMS gyroscopes used in modern AHRS sense angular rate?
choices: Magnetometers detect Earth's rotating magnetic field
Vibrating elements are deflected by the Coriolis force
Spinning rotors maintain a fixed orientation
Laser beams shift phase in an optical cavity
multipleChoice.explanation: The vibrating-beam plus Coriolis-deflection model is the defining principle of MEMS rate sensors.

practice.question: Scenario: A technician is studying an AHRS service manual. Which operating principle explains how the internal MEMS gyros sense aircraft rotation?
choices: Laser beams phase-shifting in an optical cavity
Vibrating elements deflected by the Coriolis force
practice.explanation: MEMS rate gyros use Coriolis deflection on a vibrating mass; the distractors describe RLG, mechanical gyro, and magnetometer principles.

preassessment.question: MEMS gyros sense rotation by what principle?
choices: Laser phase shift in optical cavity

questions.learningObjective: Identify the sensor that measures linear acceleration along each axis.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize the linear accelerometer as the AHRS sensor that measures straight-line motion.
questions.targetSourceStem: An AHRS contains sensors that measure straight-line motion along each axis. What type of sensor provides this measurement?

flashcard.definition: Linear accelerometers inside an AHRS measure acceleration along each axis, supporting tilt and motion calculations.
flashcard.analogy: Like the sensor in your phone that knows when you're shaking it or flipping it flat.

jeopardy.question: This is the AHRS sensor type that measures straight-line acceleration along each axis.
jeopardy.explanation: Linear accelerometers measure acceleration; rate gyros measure rotation, magnetometers sense heading, and barometric transducers measure pressure.

multipleChoice.question: Which AHRS sensor measures straight-line acceleration along each axis?
multipleChoice.explanation: Accelerometers alone measure linear motion; rate gyros, magnetometers, and pressure sensors measure rotation, heading, and altitude.

practice.question: Scenario: During an AHRS teardown, the technician identifies a sensor that outputs a signal proportional to linear acceleration. What type of sensor is it?
practice.explanation: A sensor output proportional to linear acceleration identifies it as an accelerometer; the distractors produce other outputs.

preassessment.question: Which sensor measures linear acceleration in an AHRS?

questions.learningObjective: Identify what the magnetometer provides to the AHRS.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the AHRS magnetometer measures Earth's magnetic field direction for heading computation.
questions.targetSourceStem: What measurement does the magnetometer provide to the AHRS for heading computation?

flashcard.definition: The magnetometer measures Earth's magnetic field direction, which the AHRS uses to compute magnetic heading.
flashcard.analogy: Like a tiny digital compass needle feeding the AHRS.

jeopardy.question: This is what the AHRS magnetometer provides to support heading computation.
jeopardy.explanation: Magnetometers sense Earth's field; the distractors are voltages, pressures, and fuel levels handled by other sensors.

multipleChoice.question: Which measurement does an AHRS magnetometer provide for heading calculation?
choices: Voltage level on the aircraft bus
multipleChoice.explanation: Only magnetic field direction supports heading computation; the other parameters are unrelated.

practice.question: Scenario: A technician is tracing AHRS heading data back to its source sensor. Which measurement does the magnetometer supply?
choices: Fuel quantity in each fuel tank
DC bus voltage level at the AHRS input
practice.explanation: The AHRS uses the field direction from the magnetometer to compute magnetic heading.

preassessment.question: What does the magnetometer give the AHRS?

questions.learningObjective: Identify installation-induced causes of magnetometer errors.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that DC current wiring routed near the magnetometer is a common cause of heading errors in aircraft installations.
questions.targetSourceStem: A technician is troubleshooting erratic AHRS heading. What commonly causes magnetometer errors in aircraft installations?

flashcard.definition: DC current wiring run too close to the magnetometer generates local magnetic fields that disturb AHRS heading.
flashcard.analogy: Like holding a magnet near your phone's compass and watching it spin.

jeopardy.question: This installation issue commonly causes magnetometer errors and erratic AHRS heading.
choices: DC current-carrying wiring near the sensor
Low oxygen level in the flight deck
jeopardy.explanation: Current-carrying DC wires create magnetic fields that couple into the magnetometer; the distractors affect other systems entirely.

multipleChoice.question: Which installation factor most often causes magnetometer errors in an AHRS?
choices: DC current-carrying wiring routed near the sensor
Pitot tube obstruction from insects or ice
Multipath interference on the GPS signal
Low oxygen concentration in the flight deck
multipleChoice.explanation: Magnetometers are sensitive to stray magnetic fields generated by nearby current flow; the other issues affect unrelated systems.

practice.question: Scenario: An AHRS installation produces erratic heading that worsens with high landing light current. What is the likely cause?
choices: DC current-carrying wiring is routed too close to the magnetometer
The pitot tube is partially blocked by insects
GPS satellite signal multipath is corrupting position
Low oxygen in the flight deck is affecting electronics
practice.explanation: Load-dependent heading error strongly implicates magnetic fields from nearby current flow, which is a wiring-routing issue.

preassessment.question: Common magnetometer error cause in aircraft?
choices: DC current wiring near the sensor

questions.learningObjective: Identify the sensor that measures rotation rate inside an AHRS.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize the rate gyroscope assembly as the AHRS sensor that measures angular rate for attitude computation.
questions.targetSourceStem: What type of sensor in the AHRS measures rotation rate for attitude computation?

flashcard.definition: A rate gyroscope assembly measures angular rate on each axis, feeding the AHRS attitude computation.
flashcard.analogy: Like the sensor in a VR headset that tracks how fast your head is turning.

jeopardy.question: This type of AHRS sensor measures angular rate to support attitude computation.
jeopardy.explanation: Only rate gyros output angular rate; the distractors measure temperature, pressure, or airspeed.

multipleChoice.question: Which sensor inside an AHRS measures angular rate for attitude calculation?
multipleChoice.explanation: Rate gyros provide the angular rate signal; temperature and pressure sensors do not contribute to attitude.

practice.question: Scenario: A technician is reviewing an AHRS block diagram. Which sensor is the primary source of angular rate for attitude solutions?
practice.explanation: Angular rate is the defining output of a rate gyro; other listed sensors measure unrelated parameters.

preassessment.question: Which sensor measures AHRS rotation rate?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the error characteristic of MEMS gyros over time.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize bias instability drift as the MEMS gyro error that accumulates even when the aircraft is stationary.
questions.targetSourceStem: MEMS gyroscopes accumulate error over time even when stationary. What is this error characteristic called?

flashcard.definition: Bias instability drift is a slow random walk in the zero-output level of a MEMS gyro, even when the sensor is stationary.
flashcard.analogy: Like a digital scale that slowly creeps upward even with nothing on it.

jeopardy.question: This error characteristic causes a stationary MEMS gyro to accumulate a slow random offset.
jeopardy.explanation: Bias instability is a defining MEMS limitation; the distractors relate to fiber optic gyros, environment, or pressure sensors.

multipleChoice.question: What is the name of the MEMS gyro error that grows over time even when the aircraft is stationary?
multipleChoice.explanation: Bias instability is the walk-off of the MEMS zero-rate output; other options describe unrelated phenomena.

practice.question: Scenario: During a bench test, a stationary MEMS rate gyro slowly develops a non-zero output. How should the technician describe this error?
choices: Optical beam decay inside the resonator
Bias instability drift of the MEMS sensor
Barometric hysteresis lag of the transducer
practice.explanation: Slow drift at zero input is bias instability; the other terms do not apply to MEMS rate sensors.

preassessment.question: MEMS gyro zero-error drift over time is called?

questions.learningObjective: Explain how modern AHRS calibrates heading during flight.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that modern AHRS fuses magnetometer data with GPS track to calibrate heading output in flight.
questions.targetSourceStem: How does a modern AHRS calibrate its heading output during flight operations?

flashcard.definition: Modern AHRS fuses magnetometer field data with GPS ground track to continually calibrate and stabilize heading.
flashcard.analogy: Like a GPS that keeps pointing you in the right direction by blending compass and course-over-ground data.

jeopardy.question: This fusion strategy keeps a modern AHRS heading output calibrated in flight.
choices: Reading the turn coordinator ball position
Referencing the onboard oxygen sensor output
Fusing magnetometer data with GPS track
jeopardy.explanation: Sensor fusion with GPS track is the standard technique; the other options involve unrelated systems.

multipleChoice.question: In the air, what mechanism keeps the heading solution inside a modern AHRS drifting-corrected and aligned?
choices: It references the onboard oxygen sensor output
It reads the turn coordinator ball position
It accepts manual pilot trim corrections
It fuses magnetometer output with GPS ground track
multipleChoice.explanation: Magnetometer-plus-GPS fusion reduces magnetic errors and keeps heading accurate; the distractors do not inform heading.

practice.question: Scenario: A technician must explain how a modern AHRS keeps heading accurate during flight. Which technique is used?
choices: Sampling the onboard oxygen sensor output
Reading the turn coordinator ball position
Sensor fusion of magnetometer with GPS ground track
practice.explanation: Fusing magnetic heading with GPS track is how modern AHRS remain accurate despite magnetic disturbances.

preassessment.question: How does a modern AHRS calibrate heading in flight?

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose gradual attitude drift during a long flight.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that gyro bias drift without a periodic alignment update is a likely cause of slow attitude drift on long flights.
questions.targetSourceStem: The pilot reports that displayed attitude gradually drifts from actual aircraft attitude over a long flight. What is the likely cause?

flashcard.definition: Without a periodic alignment update, gyro bias drift accumulates over long flights and pulls displayed attitude off of truth.
flashcard.analogy: Like a stopwatch that loses a second every minute and nobody resets it over an eight-hour race.

jeopardy.question: This is the likely cause when displayed attitude slowly drifts from actual aircraft attitude on a long flight.
jeopardy.explanation: Attitude is computed from rate gyros plus accelerometer references; without an alignment update, bias drift accumulates over a long flight.

multipleChoice.question: During a long flight, displayed AHRS attitude gradually diverges from actual aircraft attitude. What is the most likely cause?
choices: An air data computer altitude error
multipleChoice.explanation: Attitude computation integrates rate data and accumulates bias error; periodic alignment updates correct it. The distractors would not produce slow attitude drift.

practice.question: Scenario: After a six-hour cruise, the pilot reports the attitude indication is 4 degrees nose-low compared to actual aircraft pitch. Which cause is most likely?
choices: AHRS gyro bias drift without alignment update
An air data computer altitude calibration error
A partial blockage of the static port
practice.explanation: Slow, cumulative attitude error on long flights is characteristic of uncorrected gyro bias drift.

preassessment.question: Gradual attitude drift on long flight. Likely cause?
choices: Gyro bias drift without alignment update

questions.learningObjective: Identify the AHRS design feature enabling fast recovery after a power interruption.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that non-volatile memory storage of alignment data lets an AHRS recover attitude quickly after a power loss.
questions.targetSourceStem: After a momentary power interruption, the AHRS quickly recovers valid attitude. What design feature enables this rapid recovery?

flashcard.definition: Non-volatile memory preserves the latest alignment data so the AHRS can quickly resume valid attitude after a momentary power loss.
flashcard.analogy: Like a laptop resuming instantly from sleep because its state was saved to SSD.

jeopardy.question: This AHRS design feature supports rapid recovery of valid attitude after a power interruption.
jeopardy.explanation: Non-volatile memory retains alignment data through power loss; the distractors cannot substitute for gyro alignment data.

multipleChoice.question: Why does an AHRS recover valid attitude quickly after a momentary power interruption?
choices: The flight director recomputes the alignment data
The barometric altimeter supplies backup attitude
The magnetometer provides complete attitude output
multipleChoice.explanation: Without non-volatile storage, the AHRS would require a full alignment; magnetometers, altimeters, and flight directors cannot replace this.

practice.question: Scenario: After a brief bus transfer, the AHRS resumes valid attitude within seconds. Which design feature enabled that fast recovery?
choices: The barometric altimeter backed up attitude
The flight director recomputed the alignment
practice.explanation: Quick recovery after a power blip comes from saved alignment data in non-volatile memory.

preassessment.question: Why does AHRS recover quickly after power interruption?

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose a heading disagreement between the magnetic compass and a slaved HSI.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the flux detector and compass compensator are the first items to inspect when a slaved HSI disagrees with the magnetic compass.
questions.targetSourceStem: A pilot reports 5° disagreement between the magnetic compass and the slaved HSI in straight and level flight. What should the technician inspect?

flashcard.definition: When the slaved HSI and magnetic compass disagree in level flight, inspect the flux detector and compass compensator first.
flashcard.analogy: Like checking the thermostat sensor and the thermometer on the wall when they disagree about room temperature.

jeopardy.question: These components should be inspected first when a slaved HSI disagrees with the magnetic compass by a steady 5 degrees.
choices: The GPS antenna and cable connections
The pitot tube and static port condition
The flux detector and the compass compensator
The audio panel and headset wiring
jeopardy.explanation: Heading accuracy depends on the flux detector feeding the slaving system and compensator correction; distractors relate to unrelated systems.

multipleChoice.question: A slaved HSI differs from the magnetic compass by 5 degrees in level flight. Which components should be inspected first?
choices: The audio panel and headset wiring
The pitot tube and static port condition
The flux detector and the compass compensator
The GPS antenna and cable connections
multipleChoice.explanation: Heading source and compensator adjustment are the most likely suspects; the distractors are unrelated to heading sensing.

practice.question: Scenario: In straight and level flight, the pilot finds the slaved HSI reading 5 degrees off the whiskey compass. Where should the technician start inspection?
choices: At the GPS antenna and its cable
At the pitot tube and the static port
At the flux detector and the compass compensator
At the audio panel and the headset wiring
practice.explanation: The flux detector provides heading data to the slaving amplifier, and the compensator fine-tunes heading accuracy; both are the right first check.

preassessment.question: HSI disagrees with compass 5°. Inspect what?

questions.learningObjective: Identify which mechanical instruments AHRS replaces.
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that installing AHRS can eliminate the mechanical vertical gyro and directional gyro from the aircraft.
questions.targetSourceStem: When AHRS is installed, which traditional mechanical instruments can be removed from the aircraft?

flashcard.definition: A certified AHRS installation supplies attitude and heading data that makes the traditional vertical gyro and directional gyro redundant.
flashcard.analogy: Like installing a GPS that makes the old paper map and compass case redundant for navigation.

jeopardy.question: These two traditional mechanical instruments are replaced when a certified AHRS is installed.
choices: The airspeed indicator and the tachometer
The vertical speed and the sensitive altimeter
The sensitive altimeter and the compass
The vertical gyro and the directional gyro
jeopardy.explanation: AHRS supplies attitude and heading data previously provided by the vertical and directional gyros; VSI, altimeter, airspeed, and tach serve other functions.

multipleChoice.question: When an AHRS is installed, which legacy mechanical instruments are typically removed?
choices: The airspeed indicator and the tachometer
The vertical speed indicator and sensitive altimeter
The sensitive altimeter and the standby compass
The vertical gyro and directional gyro
multipleChoice.explanation: AHRS provides attitude and heading, replacing VG and DG; the distractors cover air data or engine instruments unrelated to AHRS functions.

practice.question: Scenario: A shop is installing a new AHRS with EFIS displays. Which mechanical instruments are eligible for removal per the STC?
choices: The airspeed indicator and the tachometer
The vertical speed indicator and the altimeter
The sensitive altimeter and the standby compass
The vertical gyro and the directional gyro
practice.explanation: AHRS fulfills the attitude and heading roles previously served by the vertical gyro and directional gyro.

preassessment.question: Which mechanical instruments does AHRS replace?

questions.learningObjective: Classify aileron, elevator, and rudder as primary flight control surfaces driven by autopilot servos.
questions.fullObjective: Given an autopilot system that commands ailerons, elevator, and rudder, the technician classifies these surfaces as primary flight controls and distinguishes them from secondary, trim, or high-lift devices.
questions.targetSourceStem: An autopilot system moves the ailerons, elevator, and rudder through servo actuators. What classification applies to these control surfaces?

flashcard.definition: The aileron, elevator, and rudder, which are driven by autopilot servos to control roll, pitch, and yaw respectively.
flashcard.analogy: Think of them as the steering wheel, gas, and brake of an aircraft — the three inputs that directly maneuver the plane in flight.

jeopardy.question: This category of control surfaces — aileron, elevator, and rudder — is what the autopilot servos actually move.
jeopardy.explanation: Aileron, elevator, and rudder are classified as primary flight controls because they directly produce roll, pitch, and yaw moments.

multipleChoice.question: Autopilot servos in a conventional aircraft drive the ailerons, elevator, and rudder. How are these surfaces categorized?
multipleChoice.explanation: Ailerons, elevator, and rudder provide roll, pitch, and yaw control and are the primary flight control surfaces.

practice.question: A technician identifies the three surfaces actuated by autopilot servos on a 3-axis system. What category do they belong to?
practice.explanation: These three surfaces are the primary flight controls; flaps and trim tabs fall into other categories.

preassessment.question: Which category includes the ailerons, elevator, and rudder driven by autopilot servos?

questions.learningObjective: Classify flaps, slats, and spoilers as secondary or auxiliary flight controls.
questions.fullObjective: Given a list of surfaces including flaps, slats, and spoilers, the technician identifies them as secondary or auxiliary flight controls used for lift augmentation and drag modulation rather than direct roll, pitch, or yaw control.
questions.targetSourceStem: During approach, the pilot extends flaps, slats, and deploys spoilers after landing. What classification applies to these surfaces?

flashcard.definition: Surfaces such as flaps, slats, and spoilers that modify lift or drag but do not directly produce roll, pitch, or yaw.
flashcard.analogy: Like adjusting the seat or mirrors in a car — they change how the vehicle performs but are not the steering wheel itself.

jeopardy.question: Flaps, slats, and spoilers all fall under this class of flight control.
jeopardy.explanation: Flaps, slats, and spoilers modify lift and drag; they are categorized as secondary or auxiliary flight controls.

multipleChoice.question: Flaps are extended for approach and spoilers are deployed after touchdown. What category do these surfaces belong to?
multipleChoice.explanation: These high-lift and drag-producing surfaces are secondary or auxiliary controls.

practice.question: A technician lists flaps, slats, and spoilers on a control-surface diagram. How should they be labeled?
practice.explanation: Flaps, slats, and spoilers modify lift and drag and are classified as secondary or auxiliary flight controls.

preassessment.question: Flaps, slats, and spoilers are examples of which type of flight control?
preassessment.explanation: Flaps, slats, and spoilers are secondary or auxiliary flight controls that modify lift and drag.

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose porpoising oscillation following pitch servo maintenance as caused by incorrect bridle cable tension.
questions.fullObjective: Given a post-maintenance report of repetitive nose-up and nose-down pitch oscillation in altitude hold, the technician recognizes this as porpoising and attributes it to improper bridle cable tension on the pitch servo.
questions.targetSourceStem: After pitch servo maintenance, the pilot reports the aircraft repeatedly pitches nose-up then nose-down during altitude hold. What is this oscillation called and what causes it?

flashcard.definition: A repetitive nose-up/nose-down pitch oscillation caused by incorrect bridle cable tension on the pitch servo after maintenance.
flashcard.analogy: Like a dolphin leaping in and out of the water — the airplane bobs because the servo cable is too loose or too tight to hold a steady pitch.

jeopardy.question: The rhythmic nose-up, nose-down oscillation seen after pitch servo work, caused by improper bridle tension.
jeopardy.explanation: Porpoising is the classic symptom of incorrect bridle cable tension at the pitch servo.

multipleChoice.question: Following recent pitch servo work, the aircraft cycles nose-up and nose-down while altitude hold is engaged. What is the name and cause?
choices: Dutch roll caused by yaw-roll coupling
Phugoid caused by long-period airspeed variation
Porpoising caused by bridle cable tension error
multipleChoice.explanation: Post-maintenance porpoising almost always traces to improperly set bridle cable tension.

practice.question: After a pitch-axis servo rig, the aircraft begins a slow up-down pitch cycle in altitude hold. What should the technician suspect?
choices: Dutch roll from swept-wing damping loss
practice.explanation: The symptom is porpoising and points to bridle cable tension that was not set to spec.

preassessment.question: A nose-up/nose-down pitch cycle appearing after pitch servo maintenance is called what, and what causes it?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the flight director computer as the source of command bars displayed on the attitude indicator.
questions.fullObjective: Given a PFD presentation of pitch and roll command bars that the pilot follows manually, the technician identifies the flight director computer as the source of those command cues.
questions.targetSourceStem: The pilot hand-flies while following pitch and roll commands shown as bars on the attitude indicator. What system generates these command cues?

flashcard.definition: The subsystem that computes pitch and roll steering commands and displays them as bars on the attitude indicator for the pilot to follow manually.
flashcard.analogy: Like a GPS with turn-by-turn arrows — the FD tells the pilot where to steer, but the pilot still holds the wheel.

jeopardy.question: This computer paints the pitch and roll command bars on the attitude indicator for the pilot to follow.
jeopardy.explanation: The flight director computes steering commands and displays them as command bars.

multipleChoice.question: A pilot hand-flies the aircraft by following bars displayed on the attitude indicator. Which unit generates those bars?
multipleChoice.explanation: The FD computer generates the command cues; the autopilot would physically move the controls.

practice.question: On a PFD, pink V-bars steer the pilot in pitch and roll. What produces these bars?
practice.explanation: The flight director computes steering solutions displayed as command bars.

preassessment.question: Which system produces the pitch and roll command bars shown on the attitude indicator?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the yaw damper as the autopilot subsystem that damps yaw-axis oscillations.
questions.fullObjective: Given a description of oscillation damping about the yaw (vertical) axis, the technician identifies the yaw damper as the responsible autopilot subsystem.
questions.targetSourceStem: What autopilot system provides damping about the vertical (yaw) axis to prevent oscillation?

flashcard.definition: The autopilot subsystem that applies rudder inputs to damp oscillation about the yaw (vertical) axis.
flashcard.analogy: Like a shock absorber on a car — it doesn't steer, it just smooths out the wiggle.

jeopardy.question: This autopilot subsystem quiets oscillations about the vertical axis.
jeopardy.explanation: The yaw damper dedicates rudder commands to damp yaw-axis oscillation.

multipleChoice.question: Which autopilot component damps oscillations about the aircraft's vertical axis?
multipleChoice.explanation: The yaw damper is purpose-built for yaw-axis oscillation suppression.

practice.question: Swept-wing aircraft rely on a subsystem to stabilize yaw oscillations. What is it called?
practice.explanation: The yaw damper controls yaw oscillations through rudder inputs.

preassessment.question: Which subsystem damps oscillation about the vertical axis?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the three primary surfaces commanded by a 3-axis autopilot: elevator, ailerons, and rudder.
questions.fullObjective: Given a 3-axis autopilot installation, the technician correctly lists the commanded surfaces as elevator, ailerons, and rudder and excludes secondary or trim surfaces.
questions.targetSourceStem: An aircraft has a 3-axis autopilot installed. Which control surfaces does this system command?

flashcard.definition: An autopilot that drives the elevator, ailerons, and rudder to command pitch, roll, and yaw.
flashcard.analogy: Like a tripod — three legs for pitch, roll, and yaw holding the aircraft steady.

jeopardy.question: These are the three surfaces a 3-axis autopilot moves.
jeopardy.explanation: Pitch, roll, and yaw axes correspond to elevator, aileron, and rudder.

multipleChoice.question: Which combination of surfaces does a 3-axis autopilot actively drive?
multipleChoice.explanation: Three-axis means pitch, roll, and yaw — elevator, ailerons, and rudder.

practice.question: A 3-axis autopilot drives which primary flight controls?
practice.explanation: All three primary flight controls are driven by a 3-axis autopilot.

preassessment.question: A 3-axis autopilot commands which surfaces?

questions.learningObjective: Interpret the abbreviation IAS on an autopilot mode annunciator as Indicated Airspeed.
questions.fullObjective: Given the abbreviation IAS displayed on an autopilot mode annunciator, the technician identifies it as Indicated Airspeed and distinguishes it from distractor expansions.
questions.targetSourceStem: The abbreviation IAS appears on the autopilot mode annunciator. What parameter does this abbreviation represent?

flashcard.definition: An autopilot mode that commands pitch to hold the airspeed read directly from the pitot-static airspeed indicator.
flashcard.analogy: Like cruise control that holds the speedometer needle steady instead of a fixed altitude.

jeopardy.question: On an autopilot annunciator, these three letters stand for this airspeed parameter.
jeopardy.explanation: IAS expands to Indicated Airspeed, the speed shown on the ASI.

multipleChoice.question: The letters IAS illuminate on the autopilot mode annunciator. What does the abbreviation stand for?
multipleChoice.explanation: IAS means Indicated Airspeed and denotes an airspeed-hold pitch mode.

practice.question: An autopilot annunciator displays IAS. How should the technician interpret this label?
practice.explanation: IAS universally refers to Indicated Airspeed on flight-deck annunciators.

preassessment.question: What does IAS mean on an autopilot annunciator?

questions.learningObjective: Recognize that lateral mode changes (heading select) do not affect the active vertical mode (altitude hold).
questions.fullObjective: Given an autopilot holding altitude while the pilot rotates the heading knob, the technician states that the vertical mode remains engaged because lateral and vertical channels operate independently.
questions.targetSourceStem: With the autopilot in altitude hold, the pilot rotates the heading knob. What effect does this have on the vertical mode?

flashcard.definition: Autopilot lateral and vertical channels operate separately, so changing a heading bug does not disturb altitude hold.
flashcard.analogy: Like changing the radio station while cruise control is on — the speed setting isn't affected.

jeopardy.question: Twisting the heading bug while altitude hold is engaged has this effect on the vertical channel.
