jeopardy.explanation: The lateral and vertical channels are independent; altitude hold is preserved.

multipleChoice.question: While altitude hold is active, the pilot turns the heading selector. What happens to the vertical mode?
multipleChoice.explanation: Changing the lateral command does not interrupt the vertical mode.

practice.question: An autopilot holding altitude receives a heading-bug input. What should the technician expect of the vertical mode?
practice.explanation: Vertical and lateral channels are decoupled in typical autopilot architectures.

preassessment.question: When the heading knob is rotated during altitude hold, what occurs in vertical mode?

questions.learningObjective: Identify a closed-loop feedback system as one that compares actual output to commanded position and corrects error.
questions.fullObjective: Given a description of continuous output measurement and error correction, the technician identifies the architecture as a closed-loop feedback control system.
questions.targetSourceStem: What type of control system continuously measures output and corrects errors by comparing actual to commanded position?

flashcard.definition: A control architecture that continuously senses actual output, compares it to the command, and drives the error to zero.
flashcard.analogy: Like a thermostat — it reads the room temperature, compares to the setpoint, and fires the furnace until they match.

jeopardy.question: This type of control system measures its output and adjusts to close any command-versus-actual error.
jeopardy.explanation: Closed-loop systems use feedback to zero out error.

multipleChoice.question: A control system monitors actual output and nulls any difference from the commanded position. What architecture is this?
multipleChoice.explanation: Closed-loop feedback matches measured output to the command.

practice.question: An autopilot servo uses a position sensor to confirm surface deflection against the commanded value. What type of system is this?
practice.explanation: Feedback of actual surface position characterizes a closed-loop system.

preassessment.question: Which control system continuously corrects error using feedback?

questions.learningObjective: Identify vertical gyro or AHRS output as the source of pitch and roll attitude reference to the autopilot.
questions.fullObjective: Given a requirement for pitch and roll attitude reference, the technician selects the vertical gyro or AHRS output as the autopilot's primary attitude source.
questions.targetSourceStem: What sensor provides the pitch and roll attitude reference signals to the autopilot computer?

flashcard.definition: The sensor unit that supplies pitch and roll attitude data to the autopilot computer.
flashcard.analogy: Like the carpenter's level that tells you if the floor is tilting — the AHRS tells the autopilot which way is up.

jeopardy.question: This sensor package delivers pitch and roll reference to the autopilot.
jeopardy.explanation: Attitude reference comes from a vertical gyro or a modern AHRS.

multipleChoice.question: Which sensor output feeds pitch and roll attitude data to the autopilot computer?
multipleChoice.explanation: Vertical gyro or AHRS supplies pitch and roll attitude.

practice.question: A technician traces pitch/roll attitude signals into the autopilot. Which source should be verified first?
practice.explanation: Attitude signal originates from the vertical gyro/AHRS.

preassessment.question: Which sensor provides pitch and roll attitude to the autopilot?
preassessment.explanation: The vertical gyro or AHRS supplies pitch and roll attitude to the autopilot; the other items serve different functions.

questions.learningObjective: Identify Dutch roll as the coupled yaw-roll oscillation that a yaw damper is designed to suppress in swept-wing aircraft.
questions.fullObjective: Given a description of yaw-roll coupled oscillation in swept-wing aircraft, the technician names Dutch roll as the phenomenon the yaw damper suppresses.
questions.targetSourceStem: The yaw damper is designed to suppress an oscillation that couples yaw and roll motions in swept-wing aircraft. What is this oscillation called?

flashcard.definition: A coupled yaw-and-roll oscillation common to swept-wing aircraft with weak natural yaw damping, suppressed by the yaw damper.
flashcard.analogy: Like a pendulum that swings side-to-side while also tilting — the two motions reinforce each other.

jeopardy.question: This yaw-and-roll coupled oscillation of swept-wing jets is exactly what the yaw damper is built to kill.
jeopardy.explanation: Dutch roll couples yaw and roll and is damped by the yaw damper.

multipleChoice.question: Which aerodynamic oscillation couples yaw and roll motions and is targeted by the yaw damper?
multipleChoice.explanation: Dutch roll is the classic yaw/roll coupling issue on swept-wing aircraft.

practice.question: An airline jet exhibits combined yaw and roll oscillation with yaw damper inoperative. What is the oscillation called?
practice.explanation: Dutch roll appears when the yaw damper is not active to suppress it.

preassessment.question: What is the yaw-roll coupled oscillation called?

questions.learningObjective: Identify yaw rate from a rate gyro as the sensor input the yaw damper uses to counter Dutch roll.
questions.fullObjective: Given a yaw damper architecture, the technician identifies yaw rate (from a rate gyro) as the primary input driving rudder commands to oppose Dutch roll.
questions.targetSourceStem: What sensor input does the yaw damper use to detect and counter Dutch roll oscillation?

flashcard.definition: Angular yaw-rate data from a rate gyro used by the yaw damper to drive opposing rudder commands.
flashcard.analogy: Like a car's stability control using the yaw-rate sensor to nudge a brake and straighten a skid.

jeopardy.question: This sensor signal tells the yaw damper how fast the nose is swinging left or right.
jeopardy.explanation: The yaw damper uses yaw-rate data to command opposing rudder.

multipleChoice.question: Which sensor signal drives the yaw damper's rudder command during Dutch roll?
choices: Yaw rate from a rate gyro
multipleChoice.explanation: Yaw-rate sensing is the classical yaw damper input.

practice.question: A yaw damper continuously applies small rudder inputs based on an angular-rate measurement. What provides that measurement?
practice.explanation: A dedicated rate gyro provides yaw-rate data to the damper.

preassessment.question: Which input does the yaw damper rely on?

questions.learningObjective: Describe the pitch servo as the actuator that applies force to move the elevator when commanded.
questions.fullObjective: Given a pitch command from the autopilot computer, the technician describes the pitch servo as the device that converts that command into elevator movement.
questions.targetSourceStem: When the autopilot computer commands a pitch change, what does the pitch servo do?

flashcard.definition: The electromechanical actuator that applies force to the elevator cable/quadrant when commanded by the autopilot computer.
flashcard.analogy: Like a motorized arm pulling the control column — the servo does the muscle work for the autopilot's brain.

jeopardy.question: This actuator supplies the muscle that moves the elevator on command.
choices: Applies force to move the elevator
jeopardy.explanation: The pitch servo is an actuator, not a sensor or signal generator.

multipleChoice.question: On receiving a pitch command, what action does the pitch servo take?
choices: It generates the pitch command signal
It applies force to move the elevator
multipleChoice.explanation: Servos are actuators — they apply torque/force to the control run.

practice.question: A technician traces the autopilot pitch loop. What is the servo's role?
choices: To apply force that moves the elevator
practice.explanation: The pitch servo actuates the elevator; sensing and command generation occur elsewhere.

preassessment.question: What does the pitch servo do on command?

questions.learningObjective: Identify attitude hold as the autopilot mode that maintains wings-level flight absent pilot input.
questions.fullObjective: Given a requirement to keep the aircraft wings-level without pilot control wheel input, the technician selects attitude hold as the correct mode.
questions.targetSourceStem: Which autopilot mode maintains wings-level flight without pilot input on the control wheel?

flashcard.definition: An autopilot mode that maintains the pitch and roll attitude existing at engagement, typically wings-level cruise.
flashcard.analogy: Like letting go of a bicycle's handlebars while pressing a 'keep it straight' button — the machine preserves current attitude.

jeopardy.question: This basic autopilot mode keeps the wings level with no wheel input.
jeopardy.explanation: Attitude hold maintains the roll attitude, typically wings level.

multipleChoice.question: Which autopilot mode is used to keep the wings level without active pilot input?
multipleChoice.explanation: Attitude hold preserves the roll attitude existing when engaged.

practice.question: A pilot releases the control wheel and wants the autopilot to keep the wings level. Which mode is appropriate?
practice.explanation: Attitude hold maintains wings level without further pilot input.

preassessment.question: Which mode holds wings level with no pilot input?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the items verified during an autopilot self-test: servo motion and warning lights.
questions.fullObjective: Given a preflight autopilot self-test sequence, the technician lists servo motion and warning-light operation as the verified functions.
questions.targetSourceStem: What functions are verified during a preflight autopilot self-test sequence?

flashcard.definition: A built-in test that confirms servo motion and warning-light operation before flight.
flashcard.analogy: Like a car's ignition check that runs the wipers and lights once to prove the circuits work.

jeopardy.question: These two items are exercised every time you press the autopilot self-test.
jeopardy.explanation: Self-tests confirm servos move and warning annunciators illuminate.

multipleChoice.question: A preflight autopilot self-test checks which pair of items?
multipleChoice.explanation: Self-tests are designed to verify servo response and fault annunciation.

practice.question: During a preflight, the autopilot runs an automatic test. What functions are verified?
practice.explanation: The preflight self-test exercises each servo and runs the annunciator-light sequence.

preassessment.question: What does the autopilot self-test verify?
preassessment.explanation: The preflight self-test exercises the servos and cycles the warning annunciator lights.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the air data computer (ADC) as the source of barometric altitude data for autopilot altitude hold.
questions.fullObjective: Given an autopilot altitude-hold mode, the technician identifies the ADC as the barometric altitude source.
questions.targetSourceStem: What system provides barometric altitude data to the autopilot for altitude hold mode?

flashcard.definition: The unit that processes pitot-static inputs and outputs barometric altitude, airspeed, and vertical speed to the autopilot.
flashcard.analogy: Like a smart weather station — it converts raw pressure into the numbers the autopilot can use.

jeopardy.question: This computer feeds barometric altitude into the autopilot so altitude hold can work.
jeopardy.explanation: The ADC processes static pressure into altitude data for the autopilot.

multipleChoice.question: Which component supplies barometric altitude to the autopilot altitude-hold mode?
multipleChoice.explanation: The ADC processes static pressure and supplies altitude data.

practice.question: A technician verifies the altitude input to the autopilot. Which box should be checked?
practice.explanation: Altitude data originates at the ADC before feeding the autopilot.

preassessment.question: Where does the autopilot get barometric altitude?

questions.learningObjective: Explain that the autopilot pitch trim system reduces elevator servo loading during prolonged climbs or descents.
questions.fullObjective: Given an extended climb or descent with autopilot engaged, the technician explains that pitch auto-trim offloads the elevator servo by moving the trim surface.
questions.targetSourceStem: What does the autopilot pitch trim system do during extended climbs or descents?

flashcard.definition: A subsystem that moves the trim surface to offload the elevator servo during sustained pitch changes.
flashcard.analogy: Like adjusting the seat height so you don't have to keep holding yourself up — reduces the sustained load.

jeopardy.question: During long climbs or descents, auto-trim does this to the elevator servo.
jeopardy.explanation: Auto-trim offloads steady-state force from the elevator servo.

multipleChoice.question: How does the autopilot pitch trim system assist the elevator servo during sustained pitch changes?
multipleChoice.explanation: Auto-trim shifts the steady load from the servo to the trim surface.

practice.question: During an autopilot-commanded 5,000-foot climb, what role does pitch trim play?
practice.explanation: Auto-trim offloads sustained elevator force from the servo during long climbs, preventing servo saturation.

preassessment.question: What does autopilot pitch trim do during long climbs/descents?

questions.learningObjective: Explain that autopilot pitch trim should auto-disconnect when trim runs beyond a time or position limit.
questions.fullObjective: Given pitch-trim monitoring logic, the technician explains that exceeding a time or position limit triggers automatic disconnect as a runaway-prevention feature.
questions.targetSourceStem: Under what condition should the autopilot pitch trim automatically disconnect?

flashcard.definition: Logic that disconnects pitch trim when it runs beyond an allowed time or position limit, preventing runaway trim.
flashcard.analogy: Like a circuit breaker that trips if a motor runs too long — it prevents damage before it compounds.

jeopardy.question: This is the trigger that disconnects autopilot pitch trim automatically.
choices: Trim runs beyond time or position limit
jeopardy.explanation: Trim monitors disconnect when time or position limits are exceeded.

multipleChoice.question: Which scenario causes the autopilot pitch trim to auto-disconnect?
choices: Trim runs beyond its time or position limit
multipleChoice.explanation: Runaway-trim protection disconnects when time/position thresholds are crossed.

practice.question: A trim monitor circuit watches the pitch trim for which fault before disconnecting?
practice.explanation: The trim monitor watches for a runaway that exceeds the allowed time or position envelope, then disconnects before the trim reaches an unrecoverable position.

preassessment.question: When should pitch trim auto-disconnect?
preassessment.explanation: Pitch-trim auto-disconnect fires when the trim runs past its allowed time or position envelope, signaling runaway.

questions.learningObjective: Distinguish the flight director (which displays steering commands) from the autopilot (which physically moves controls).
questions.fullObjective: Given a comparison between FD and autopilot operation, the technician states that the FD displays command cues while the autopilot actuates the control surfaces.
questions.targetSourceStem: What is the fundamental difference between flight director and autopilot operation?

flashcard.definition: The flight director displays steering commands for the pilot to follow; the autopilot uses servos to move the controls automatically.
flashcard.analogy: The FD is like GPS turn arrows; the autopilot is like the car actually steering itself.

jeopardy.question: This is the core operational difference between FD and autopilot.
choices: FD uses GPS; AP uses inertial
FD runs pitch; AP runs roll
FD works climb; AP works cruise
FD shows commands; AP actuates controls
jeopardy.explanation: The FD is advisory; the autopilot is active.

multipleChoice.question: Which statement best captures how flight director operation differs from autopilot operation?
choices: The FD uses GPS and the autopilot uses inertial data
The FD controls pitch while the autopilot controls roll
The FD is used in climb and the autopilot in cruise
The FD displays commands while the autopilot moves controls
multipleChoice.explanation: The FD shows steering cues; the autopilot actuates the servos.

practice.question: A pilot engages the FD without coupling the autopilot. What happens?
choices: Only pitch is controlled, not roll
Command bars appear; the pilot hand-flies
practice.explanation: FD alone displays commands; the pilot supplies the muscle.

preassessment.question: What distinguishes FD from autopilot operation?
preassessment.explanation: The flight director displays command bars; the autopilot engages servos to move the flight controls.

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose a nonresponsive pitch axis as likely caused by a servo clutch jam or feedback failure.
questions.fullObjective: Given a pitch axis that fails to respond to autopilot commands, the technician identifies servo clutch jam or feedback failure as the most probable cause.
questions.targetSourceStem: During troubleshooting, the pitch axis will not respond to commands. What is the likely cause?

flashcard.term: Pitch servo clutch jam / feedback fail
flashcard.definition: A stuck servo clutch or loss of feedback signal that prevents the pitch axis from responding to commands.
flashcard.analogy: Like a steering wheel that turns without moving the tires — the command is there, but the connection is broken.

jeopardy.question: A pitch axis that won't move on command usually has this wrong with it.
jeopardy.explanation: A pitch axis that won't move on command is caused by a mechanical clutch jam or loss of position feedback at the servo.

multipleChoice.question: What is the most probable cause of an unresponsive autopilot pitch axis?
choices: Servo clutch jam or feedback failure
multipleChoice.explanation: Pitch-axis non-response points first to servo clutch or feedback issues.

practice.question: A technician finds the pitch servo powered but the elevator will not move. What is the likely fault?
practice.explanation: Servo clutch/feedback faults best match the observed behavior.

preassessment.question: Pitch axis won't respond — likely cause?
preassessment.explanation: A servo clutch jam or feedback failure disables pitch response.

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose instant autopilot disconnect on engagement as most likely caused by a sensor mismatch or sync failure.
questions.fullObjective: Given immediate autopilot disconnect after pressing engage, the technician attributes this to a sensor mismatch or synchronization failure at engagement.
questions.targetSourceStem: The autopilot disconnects immediately after the pilot presses the engage button. What is the most likely cause?

flashcard.definition: A mismatch between commanded and measured reference values that aborts autopilot engagement.
flashcard.analogy: Like cruise control refusing to set because the car doesn't know its own speed — the system aborts to prevent a bad command.

jeopardy.question: The autopilot disconnects the instant you press engage — this is the top suspect.
jeopardy.explanation: Immediate disconnect indicates an internal sensor sync check failed.

multipleChoice.question: What is the most probable cause of an autopilot that disconnects the moment it is engaged?
choices: Static port has a small leak
multipleChoice.explanation: Sensor cross-check failures at engagement abort the mode immediately.

practice.question: A pilot presses the autopilot engage button and it drops off within a second. What should the technician investigate first?
practice.explanation: An engage-and-drop within a second points to a sensor mismatch or sync fault between the reference sensors and the autopilot.

preassessment.question: Autopilot disconnects on engage — likely cause?
preassessment.explanation: Instant disconnect on engage points to a sensor mismatch or sync fault between the reference sensors and the autopilot.

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose persistent roll oscillation in heading mode as caused by excessive roll gain or a roll-feedback fault.
questions.fullObjective: Given an aircraft that oscillates left/right in heading mode without stabilizing, the technician attributes the behavior to high roll gain or a roll-axis feedback fault.
questions.targetSourceStem: After engaging heading mode, the aircraft continuously rolls left then right, never stabilizing on the selected heading. What is the likely cause?

flashcard.definition: An overly high roll gain or corrupted roll feedback causes sustained oscillation around the target heading.
flashcard.analogy: Like over-corrective steering on a bicycle — the more you wobble, the worse it gets when the gain is too high.

jeopardy.question: Endless left-right roll chasing a heading usually means this is wrong.
choices: Heading bug sync error on engage
Roll gain too high or feedback fault
jeopardy.explanation: Sustained oscillation points to gain or feedback issues on the roll axis.

multipleChoice.question: An aircraft in heading mode rolls left then right without settling on the selected heading. What is the likely cause?
choices: Heading bug sync error at engagement
Excess roll gain or a roll-feedback fault
Airspeed is below minimum for mode
multipleChoice.explanation: Oscillation in a servo loop indicates gain or feedback problems.

practice.question: The pilot selects heading mode and the aircraft cycles through left/right roll indefinitely. Root cause?
practice.explanation: Persistent left/right oscillation in heading mode points to excessive roll gain or a bad feedback signal.

preassessment.question: Unending left/right roll in heading mode — cause?
preassessment.explanation: Continuous left-right rolling in heading mode is caused by excessive roll gain or a bad feedback signal.

questions.learningObjective: Identify AHRS and ADC data inputs as the first items to verify when flight director command bars are missing.
questions.fullObjective: Given missing FD bars with FD selected ON, the technician verifies AHRS and ADC data inputs to the FD computer before deeper diagnosis.
questions.targetSourceStem: The flight director command bars are missing from the PFD even with FD selected ON. What should the technician verify first?

flashcard.definition: AHRS (attitude) and ADC (air data) signals are required for the FD to produce command bars.
flashcard.analogy: Like a car's GPS needing both a satellite fix and speed data — missing either input leaves the route arrows blank.

jeopardy.question: Missing FD bars send you first to this pair of input signals.
choices: AHRS and ADC data to the FD
jeopardy.explanation: AHRS and ADC data are prerequisites for FD command generation.

multipleChoice.question: The FD is selected ON but command bars are absent. What should be verified first?
choices: AHRS and ADC data inputs to FD
multipleChoice.explanation: Without valid attitude/air data, the FD cannot draw command bars.

practice.question: A technician investigates missing FD bars. What is the first check?
practice.explanation: Validate AHRS/ADC inputs before software or power troubleshooting.

preassessment.question: Missing FD bars — first check?

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose combined roll/yaw instability with heading wander as a degraded yaw-rate or heading reference signal.
questions.fullObjective: Given unstable roll and yaw with heading wander while engaged, the technician identifies a degraded yaw-rate or heading reference signal as the likely root cause.
questions.targetSourceStem: With autopilot engaged, both roll and yaw control are unstable—the aircraft wanders instead of holding heading. What signal is likely degraded?

flashcard.definition: Signals used to stabilize yaw and maintain heading; degradation causes wander and unstable roll/yaw.
flashcard.analogy: Like a compass needle that jitters — the autopilot can't lock on if its reference is noisy.

jeopardy.question: This signal, when degraded, causes both roll and yaw instability and heading wander.
jeopardy.explanation: Heading and yaw-rate signal health drives roll/yaw stability.

multipleChoice.question: Roll and yaw both hunt and the aircraft wanders off heading. Which signal is most likely degraded?
choices: Yaw rate or heading reference signal
multipleChoice.explanation: Yaw-rate/heading reference problems produce exactly these symptoms.

practice.question: A technician observes both roll/yaw instability and heading drift. What input should be examined?
practice.explanation: Heading reference degradation explains the combined roll/yaw symptoms.

preassessment.question: Unstable roll/yaw with heading wander — degraded signal?
preassessment.explanation: Unstable roll/yaw with heading wander points to a degraded yaw rate or heading reference feeding the autopilot.

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose altitude-hold pitch hunting during descent as pitch-gain error or an altitude input fault.
questions.fullObjective: Given pitch hunting around target altitude during descent in altitude hold, the technician identifies pitch gain error or an altitude input fault as the most likely cause.
questions.targetSourceStem: During descent in altitude hold, the aircraft pitch hunts up and down around the target altitude without capturing. What is the most likely cause?

flashcard.definition: Excessive pitch gain or a noisy altitude input prevents stable capture, causing pitch hunt around the target altitude.
flashcard.analogy: Like trying to park a car with over-sensitive brakes and a flickering speedometer — you overshoot back and forth.

jeopardy.question: If the aircraft porpoises around the target altitude instead of capturing, this is the primary suspect.
choices: Pitch gain error or altitude input fault
jeopardy.explanation: Hunting during altitude capture points to gain or altitude signal issues.

multipleChoice.question: What is the most likely cause of altitude-hold hunting during a descent capture?
choices: Pitch gain error or altitude input fault
multipleChoice.explanation: Pitch gain or altitude data quality control whether capture is clean.

practice.question: On a descent, the aircraft can't settle on target altitude — it oscillates vertically. What is the top suspect?
practice.explanation: Oscillation on target altitude during descent is caused by a pitch gain error or a bad altitude input to the pitch loop.

preassessment.question: Altitude-hold hunting during descent — cause?
preassessment.explanation: Altitude-hold hunting during descent traces to a pitch gain error or a bad altitude input to the pitch loop.

questions.learningObjective: State that a Garmin Emergency Autoland system uses four total servos during an autonomous landing.
questions.fullObjective: Given a Garmin Emergency Autoland installation, the technician identifies the total servo count as four.
questions.targetSourceStem: How many total servos does a Garmin Emergency Autoland system use to control the aircraft during an autonomous landing?

flashcard.definition: Garmin's Emergency Autoland system uses four total servos for autonomous aircraft control through landing.
flashcard.analogy: Like a car needing four wheels — four servos give EAL full authority over pitch, roll, yaw, and power/brakes.

jeopardy.question: This is the total servo count Garmin's Autoland system uses to land the airplane on its own.
jeopardy.explanation: Garmin Autoland uses four total servos.

multipleChoice.question: How many servos total does the Garmin Emergency Autoland system employ during an autonomous landing?
multipleChoice.explanation: The Garmin Emergency Autoland architecture uses four servos.

practice.question: A tech documents the Garmin Autoland servo count on a sign-off sheet. What number is correct?
practice.explanation: Four servos total; none of the other counts apply.

preassessment.question: Garmin Emergency Autoland servo count?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the two EAL activation triggers: pilot inactivity or a manual Autoland button press.
questions.fullObjective: Given EAL activation logic, the technician names pilot inactivity (incapacitation detection) and a manual Autoland button press as the two initiating events.
questions.targetSourceStem: What two events can initiate an Emergency Autoland activation sequence?

flashcard.definition: Emergency Autoland starts when it detects pilot inactivity or when the Autoland button is manually pressed.
flashcard.analogy: Like a panic alarm — someone hits the button, or the system notices nobody is responding and triggers on its own.

jeopardy.question: These two events kick off an Emergency Autoland sequence.
choices: Pilot inactivity or Autoland button press
High pilot stress and rough air
jeopardy.explanation: Inactivity detection or manual button press trigger Autoland.

multipleChoice.question: Which two events begin the EAL activation sequence?
choices: High pilot stress and rough air
Pilot inactivity or pressing the Autoland button
multipleChoice.explanation: These are the two designed triggers for EAL activation.

practice.question: A pilot becomes unresponsive for an extended period. What will cause EAL to engage?
practice.explanation: Inactivity detection triggers EAL automatically; the Autoland button provides manual activation. Either path engages the emergency landing sequence.

preassessment.question: Which two events initiate EAL?

questions.learningObjective: List the factors Autoland weighs when selecting a landing airport: traffic, weather, fuel, and landing distance.
questions.fullObjective: Given an EAL airport-selection scenario, the technician lists traffic, weather, fuel, and landing distance as the decision factors the system evaluates.
questions.targetSourceStem: When selecting a landing airport, the Autoland system evaluates multiple factors. Which set of factors does the system consider?

flashcard.definition: Autoland evaluates traffic, weather, fuel reserves, and required landing distance when choosing a destination.
flashcard.analogy: Like a dispatcher balancing everything at once — it's not just the closest airport but the best overall fit.

jeopardy.question: These four factors shape Autoland's airport choice.
jeopardy.explanation: Autoland weighs all four inputs, not just proximity.

multipleChoice.question: Which group of factors does Autoland consider when selecting a landing airport?
choices: Traffic, weather, fuel, and landing distance
multipleChoice.explanation: Garmin Autoland uses a multi-factor decision model.

practice.question: EAL picks a destination using a weighted evaluation. Which factor set does it use?
practice.explanation: The system considers traffic, weather, fuel, and runway suitability.

preassessment.question: EAL airport-selection factors?

questions.learningObjective: Describe the EAL relay interface panel's role as the component that configures aircraft systems for landing.
questions.fullObjective: Given an EAL event, the technician identifies the relay interface panel as the element that configures the aircraft systems for the autonomous landing.
questions.targetSourceStem: What function does the EAL relay interface panel perform during an Emergency Autoland event?

flashcard.definition: The panel that orchestrates aircraft system configuration (gear, flaps, deice, etc.) during Emergency Autoland.
flashcard.analogy: Like a smart home hub that flips all the right switches — the relay panel sets the aircraft up for landing.

jeopardy.question: This panel configures the aircraft systems during an Autoland event.
jeopardy.explanation: The relay interface panel performs the aircraft system configuration.

multipleChoice.question: Throughout an Emergency Autoland sequence, what is the job of the EAL relay interface panel inside the aircraft systems chain?
choices: It configures the aircraft systems for landing
multipleChoice.explanation: Its primary job is aircraft system configuration via relay switching.

practice.question: During an Autoland event, which unit actuates the aircraft's systems to get them ready for landing?
practice.explanation: The relay panel performs the configuration actions.

preassessment.question: EAL relay interface panel function?
preassessment.explanation: The EAL relay interface panel drives the relays that configure aircraft systems (gear, flaps, deice, firewall, air data) for landing.

questions.learningObjective: Describe how EAL brake servos connect mechanically to the hydraulic brake master cylinders to stop the aircraft.
questions.fullObjective: Given an EAL landing, the technician describes the brake servos as connecting to the hydraulic brake master cylinders to apply braking.
questions.targetSourceStem: How do the EAL brake servos bring the aircraft to a stop after touchdown?

flashcard.definition: Servos mechanically attached to the hydraulic brake master cylinders, applying brake pressure to decelerate the aircraft after touchdown.
flashcard.analogy: Like a robot foot pressing the brake pedal — the servo pushes the master cylinder just as a pilot's foot would.

jeopardy.question: This is how EAL brake servos stop the airplane after touchdown.
choices: They drive the hydraulic brake master cylinders
They clamp onto the brake assembly
They apply reverse thrust via engines
jeopardy.explanation: Brake servos connect to the hydraulic master cylinders.

multipleChoice.question: How do EAL brake servos stop the aircraft after touchdown?
choices: They connect to the hydraulic brake master cylinders
They electronically modulate the emergency brakes
They clamp directly onto the brake assembly
They apply reverse thrust through the engines
multipleChoice.explanation: Brake servos drive the hydraulic brake master cylinders.

practice.question: A technician traces the EAL braking path to its mechanical interface. What does it connect to?
practice.explanation: The servo interface is the brake master cylinder.

preassessment.question: EAL brake servos connect to what?

questions.learningObjective: Describe that after Autoland stops the aircraft on the runway, the system shuts down the engines.
questions.fullObjective: Given a completed EAL landing and stop, the technician states that the system shuts down the engines on the runway.
questions.targetSourceStem: After the Autoland system lands the aircraft and brings it to a stop, what happens next?

flashcard.definition: After stopping on the runway, the Autoland system shuts the engines down so first responders can safely approach.
flashcard.analogy: Like a self-driving car that parks and turns itself off — no further motion, safe for rescuers.

jeopardy.question: This is the Autoland system's final act after the airplane is stopped.
choices: It holds position until emergency crews arrive
It shuts the engines down on the runway
It taxis to a safe location
It taxis off the runway then stops
jeopardy.explanation: Autoland shuts down the engines after coming to a stop.

multipleChoice.question: What does Autoland do once the aircraft is stopped on the runway?
choices: It taxis to a safe location
It holds position with engines running
It taxis off the runway then stops
multipleChoice.explanation: Engine shutdown is the final, safety-critical action.

practice.question: A technician reviews EAL sequence-of-events logs. What is logged after 'full stop'?
practice.explanation: The engines are commanded off once the aircraft stops.

preassessment.question: After EAL stops the aircraft, what's next?

questions.learningObjective: List the aircraft systems EAL configures: landing gear, flaps, deice, firewall shutoff, and air data.
questions.fullObjective: Given an EAL landing sequence, the technician lists the configured systems as landing gear, flaps, deice, firewall shutoff, and air data.
questions.targetSourceStem: What aircraft systems must the EAL configure in preparation for an autonomous landing?

flashcard.definition: Landing gear, flaps, deice, firewall shutoff, and air data — all configured by EAL to prepare the aircraft for autonomous landing.
flashcard.analogy: Like a pre-landing checklist that runs itself — flaps, gear, heat, fuel cutoff, pitot, all handled automatically.

jeopardy.question: This full list of systems is what EAL sets up before landing.
jeopardy.explanation: Autoland configures the full set: gear, flaps, deice, firewall shutoff, and air data.

multipleChoice.question: To ready the airplane for an automatic touchdown during EAL, what group of airframe subsystems does the logic reconfigure?
choices: Only the landing gear and wing flaps
Gear, flaps, deice, firewall, air data
multipleChoice.explanation: EAL configures the full list: landing gear, flaps, deice, firewall shutoff, and air data.

practice.question: A tech writes the EAL preparation steps on a documentation form. What list belongs there?
practice.explanation: EAL preparation includes the full canonical list: gear, flaps, deice, firewall shutoff, and air data.

preassessment.question: Which systems does EAL configure?
preassessment.explanation: EAL configures the full landing set: gear, flaps, deice, firewall shutoff, and air data.

questions.learningObjective: State that EAL throttle servos are installed in the pedestal and mechanically connected to the throttle levers.
questions.fullObjective: Given an EAL throttle servo installation, the technician describes it as located in the pedestal with a mechanical connection to the throttle levers.
questions.targetSourceStem: Where are the EAL throttle servos physically installed in the aircraft?

flashcard.definition: Installed in the pedestal and mechanically connected to the throttle levers to adjust power during EAL.
flashcard.analogy: Like a robot arm mounted in the center console — it grabs the throttle and moves it exactly like a pilot.

jeopardy.question: This is where you'll find the EAL throttle servo and what it connects to.
choices: In the avionics bay, linked to the relay panel
In the nacelles, linked to fuel controls
In the wing roots, with independent linkage
In the pedestal, mechanically linked to throttle levers
jeopardy.explanation: Throttle servos live in the pedestal and drive the throttle levers.

multipleChoice.question: For the EAL throttle servo pair, where is the hardware mounted in the airframe and what does it actuate?
choices: In the avionics bay connected to the relay panel
In the engine nacelles connected to fuel controls
In the wing roots with independent throttle linkage
In the pedestal, mechanically connected to the throttle levers
multipleChoice.explanation: Pedestal-mounted throttle servos drive the existing throttle levers.

practice.question: A technician opens the pedestal during an EAL install. What components are present there?
choices: Throttle servos linked to throttle levers
practice.explanation: The throttle servos mount in the pedestal area.

preassessment.question: EAL throttle servo location?

questions.learningObjective: Identify the Garmin G1000 NXi flight deck with GFC 700 autopilot as the required avionics basis for EAL.
questions.fullObjective: Given an EAL installation requirement, the technician names the Garmin G1000 NXi flight deck with GFC 700 autopilot as the required avionics platform.
questions.targetSourceStem: Which avionics platform is required as the basis for an Emergency Autoland installation?

flashcard.definition: The Garmin G1000 NXi flight deck paired with the GFC 700 digital autopilot forms the basis for Emergency Autoland.
flashcard.analogy: Like a specific model of phone required to run a specific app — EAL only works on G1000 NXi + GFC 700.

jeopardy.question: This is the required avionics platform for a Garmin Autoland installation.
jeopardy.explanation: Emergency Autoland is certified on G1000 NXi with GFC 700.

multipleChoice.question: Which Garmin flight-deck and autopilot combination is required for Emergency Autoland?
choices: G1000 NXi flight deck with GFC 700 autopilot
TXi flight deck with GFC 600 autopilot
multipleChoice.explanation: EAL requires the G1000 NXi + GFC 700 combination.

practice.question: A sales quote lists EAL as an option. Which avionics platform is mandatory?
practice.explanation: Only the G1000 NXi paired with the GFC 700 supports EAL.

preassessment.question: Required avionics for EAL?
preassessment.explanation: EAL requires a G1000 NXi integrated flight deck paired with a GFC 700 autopilot.

questions.learningObjective: State that Emergency Autoland is approved for emergency use only and not as a general-purpose convenience.
questions.fullObjective: Given a scenario where a pilot wants to use EAL for fatigue management, the technician states that EAL is approved only for emergency situations.
questions.targetSourceStem: A pilot wants to use the Autoland system because they are fatigued on a long flight. Is this an approved use of the system?

flashcard.definition: Emergency Autoland is a safety-critical system approved only for genuine emergency (pilot-incapacitation) use.
flashcard.analogy: Like a car's airbag — it's reserved for crashes, not everyday driving convenience.

jeopardy.question: This is the simple rule governing when EAL may be used.
choices: When the copilot seat is empty
jeopardy.explanation: EAL is explicitly reserved for emergency situations.

multipleChoice.question: Is activating Autoland because the pilot is fatigued an approved use of the system?
choices: Yes, if the copilot seat is empty
No, EAL is for emergency situations only
Yes, it can be used for any reason
No, but practice approaches are allowed
multipleChoice.explanation: EAL is limited to emergency use.

practice.question: A customer asks a technician if Autoland can be used just to rest on a long leg. What is the correct answer?
choices: Yes if the copilot seat is empty
practice.explanation: EAL cannot be used for non-emergency convenience.

preassessment.question: Is fatigue an approved use for EAL?

questions.learningObjective: State that EAL communicates with ATC and transmits an emergency transponder code.
questions.fullObjective: Given EAL communication capabilities, the technician states that the system both communicates with ATC and sets an emergency transponder code.
questions.targetSourceStem: Does the Emergency Autoland system have the ability to communicate with air traffic control?

flashcard.definition: EAL communicates with ATC and simultaneously squawks an emergency transponder code to clear airspace.
flashcard.analogy: Like an emergency dispatcher radioing for help while also triggering a silent alarm — both channels open at once.

jeopardy.question: EAL uses both of these channels to announce the emergency to ATC.
choices: Voice messages to airport tower only
ATC voice and emergency squawk code
jeopardy.explanation: EAL talks to ATC and squawks the emergency transponder code simultaneously.

multipleChoice.question: Does Emergency Autoland provide ATC communication, and if so, how?
choices: No, it only sets a transponder emergency code
Yes, it transmits voice messages only to the tower
Yes, it transmits to ATC and squawks emergency
No, it only triggers the emergency locator beacon
multipleChoice.explanation: EAL transmits to ATC over the radio and simultaneously sets the emergency transponder code.

practice.question: During an EAL event, what does the system do on the comm/xpdr side?
choices: Talks to ATC and squawks emergency
practice.explanation: Both ATC communication and emergency squawk are active.

preassessment.question: Does EAL talk to ATC?
preassessment.explanation: EAL both transmits to ATC and sets an emergency transponder code.

questions.learningObjective: Distinguish CMS components from aircraft electrical system components.
questions.fullObjective: Given a list of aircraft equipment, identify which item belongs to the aircraft electrical system rather than the cabin management system.

flashcard.front: A shop tech lists items installed in a business jet cabin. Which one belongs to the electrical system, not the CMS?
flashcard.back: The generator control unit is electrical power infrastructure, not CMS equipment.

jeopardy.clue: This power-regulation LRU lives in the electrical system, even though a tech may mistake it for a CMS box.

multipleChoice.question: A technician reviews equipment installed on a business jet. Which component belongs to the aircraft electrical system rather than the cabin management system?
choices: The generator control unit regulates bus power.
The audio and video distribution unit routes media.
The cabin central processing unit runs CMS software.
The cabin lighting controller drives LED dimmers.

practice.scenario: You are inventorying LRUs during a pre-installation walk-through on a Challenger 350. The list includes a generator control unit, an audio and video distribution unit, a cabin central processing unit, and a lighting controller.
practice.question: Which LRU on the list is part of the aircraft electrical system rather than the cabin management system?
choices: The generator control unit is electrical system equipment.
The audio and video distribution unit is electrical equipment.
The cabin central processor is electrical system hardware.
The cabin lighting controller is electrical system gear.

preassessment.question: Which of these LRUs is part of the aircraft electrical system instead of the CMS?
choices: The generator control unit is an electrical system LRU.
The A/V distribution unit is an electrical system LRU.
The CMS central processor is an electrical system LRU.
The lighting controller is an electrical system LRU.

questions.learningObjective: Identify troubleshooting actions that violate systematic methodology on an IFE fault.
questions.fullObjective: Given an IFE troubleshooting scenario, select the action that violates systematic fault-isolation principles.

flashcard.front: Which IFE troubleshooting action violates systematic fault-isolation principles?
flashcard.back: Swapping LRUs before diagnosing the fault; replacement happens after isolation, not before.

jeopardy.clue: Doing this before actually diagnosing an IFE fault turns troubleshooting into parts roulette.
choices: Reading fault codes from the diagnostic port
Swapping LRUs before diagnosing the fault
Confirming the pilot report with the crew
Verifying power and ground at the connector

multipleChoice.question: An IFE unit is inoperative. Which troubleshooting action violates systematic fault-isolation principles?
choices: Verifying the crew-reported symptom first confirms the issue.
Swapping LRUs before the fault is diagnosed wastes parts.
Reviewing the diagnostic interface shows stored fault codes.
Checking power and ground at the LRU connector isolates supply faults.

practice.scenario: A Falcon 7X pilot reports that the aft IFE screen is black while the forward screen works. You have 45 minutes before the next departure.
practice.question: Which troubleshooting action would violate systematic fault-isolation principles?
choices: Measuring power and ground at the connector is proper.
Swapping LRUs before the fault is isolated is improper technique.
Pulling stored fault codes from the diagnostic port is proper.
Confirming the reported symptom first is proper technique.

preassessment.question: Which IFE troubleshooting action violates systematic fault-isolation principles?
choices: Reading stored fault codes is systematic.
Replacing LRUs before diagnosis is non-systematic.
Confirming crew complaint first is systematic.
Testing connector power and ground is systematic.

questions.learningObjective: Identify shortcuts during CMS software updates that risk permanent configuration loss.
questions.fullObjective: Given a CMS software-update scenario, identify which shortcut creates risk of permanent loss of customer configuration.

flashcard.front: During a quick-turn CMS software update, which shortcut risks permanent loss of the customer configuration?
flashcard.back: Skipping the configuration backup step; without a backup, restoring settings after a failed load is impossible.

jeopardy.clue: Skipping this save step before a CMS load can erase the owner's cabin settings for good.

multipleChoice.question: A tech is preparing a CMS software update on a tight schedule. Which shortcut creates a risk of permanent configuration loss?
choices: Powering down before inserting media protects interfaces.
Using manufacturer-approved media reduces install errors.
Skipping the configuration backup step removes any restore path.

practice.scenario: A Gulfstream G650 is on a quick-turn and needs a CMS software update in 30 minutes. The owner's custom scene presets and wallpaper are stored only on the CPU.
practice.question: Which shortcut during the update would create a risk of permanent configuration loss?
choices: Verifying hardware compatibility risks permanent loss of data.
Using approved media risks permanent loss of customer data.
Skipping the backup step risks permanent loss of customer data.
Powering down before media insert risks permanent loss.

preassessment.question: Which CMS update shortcut risks permanent loss of the customer configuration?
choices: Verifying hardware compatibility creates loss risk.
Using approved install media creates loss risk.
Skipping the configuration backup creates loss risk.
Powering down before media insertion creates loss risk.

questions.learningObjective: Identify wiring actions that increase EMI on CMS displays.
questions.fullObjective: Given a CMS EMI complaint, identify which technician action would increase rather than reduce the interference.

flashcard.front: A crew reports EMI on CMS displays. Which technician action would likely make the interference worse?
flashcard.back: Routing data cables near relay junction boxes; switching transients inject noise onto nearby conductors.

jeopardy.clue: Running CMS cabling right next to these switching boxes bathes data lines in transient EMI.

multipleChoice.question: The crew logs a squawk that EMI is disrupting CMS screens. Out of the troubleshooting steps below, which one would actually make the problem worse?
choices: Moving cables away from radar waveguide runs reduces pickup.
Verifying shield and bond integrity reduces coupled noise.
Locating the noise source with a spectrum analyzer helps isolate it.
Routing CMS cables close to relay junction boxes adds noise coupling.

practice.scenario: On a Hawker 800XP, the crew complains of horizontal bars and flicker on CMS displays whenever the galley loads cycle. You are planning wire-routing changes.
practice.question: Which action would most likely increase rather than decrease the interference?
choices: Separating data cables from radar waveguide reduces EMI.
Checking shielding and bonding integrity would reduce EMI.
Using a spectrum analyzer to find the source reduces EMI.
Running data cables close to relay junction boxes increases EMI.

preassessment.question: Which technician action on CMS wiring would increase EMI on the displays?
choices: Checking shield and ground integrity decreases EMI.
Moving cables away from radar waveguide decreases EMI.
Using a spectrum analyzer helps decrease EMI.
Routing cables near relay boxes increases EMI exposure.

questions.learningObjective: Recognize that testing a single CMS subsystem does not validate the whole system.
questions.fullObjective: Given a partial CMS diagnostic routine, explain why running BIT on only one subsystem fails to validate the entire CMS.

flashcard.front: A tech runs BIT only on CMS lighting. Why is this approach incomplete?
flashcard.back: The other CMS subsystems (IFE, A/V, seat, climate) stay untested and may still harbor faults.

jeopardy.clue: Running BIT on only cabin lighting leaves these other CMS areas still in the dark.

multipleChoice.question: During routine maintenance a tech runs built-in test only on the CMS lighting. Why is this approach incomplete?
choices: The other CMS subsystems remain untested and potentially faulted.
Built-in test cannot detect any lighting faults reliably.
Lighting is not actually part of the CMS function set.
Lighting should always be the last subsystem to be tested.

practice.scenario: You sign off a CMS return-to-service after running built-in test on lighting only. The next flight reveals a failed A/V distribution unit.
practice.question: Why was your diagnostic routine incomplete?
choices: Other CMS subsystems were not tested for faults.
BIT is incapable of detecting any lighting faults.
Lighting is not a function of the CMS at all.
Lighting must always be tested last in sequence.

preassessment.question: Why is running BIT only on CMS lighting an incomplete approach?
choices: Other CMS subsystems remain unverified by the test.
BIT cannot identify any lighting-related faults.
Lighting is not considered a CMS function.
Lighting belongs at the end of the test sequence.

questions.learningObjective: Identify CMS wireless settings that create security vulnerabilities.
questions.fullObjective: Given a CMS wireless-security setup, identify which configuration choice creates an attack surface.

Enabling unused wireless features expands the attack surface.

practice.question: Which configuration choice would create a security vulnerability?
choices: Enabling WPA3 encryption on the Wi-Fi hardens security.
Leaving unused wireless features enabled creates vulnerability.
Enabling AES-256 on the Bluetooth link hardens security.
Using a unique SSID with a strong password hardens security.

preassessment.question: Which CMS wireless setting creates a security vulnerability?
choices: Applying WPA3 on Wi-Fi hardens the link.
Leaving unused features active creates vulnerability.
Applying AES-256 on Bluetooth hardens the link.
Using a unique SSID and strong password hardens the link.

questions.learningObjective: Identify items unrelated to CMS display failures.

Check touch calibration at the screen surface first.
Check software version in the diagnostic menu first.

questions.learningObjective: State the primary services provided by a business aircraft connectivity system.
questions.fullObjective: Given a list of possible services, identify those provided to passengers and crew by a business aircraft connectivity system.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the data protocol used for high-speed CMS device communication.

flashcard.back: Ethernet-based networking, which provides the bandwidth needed for streaming A/V and control traffic.

multipleChoice.question: On contemporary business aircraft, what networking technology enables rapid communication among cabin devices connected to the CMS?
choices: Ethernet-based networking carries high-speed cabin data.
MIL-STD-1553 data bus carries high-speed cabin data.
ARINC 429 twisted pair carries high-speed cabin data.
RS-422 serial interface carries high-speed cabin data.

practice.scenario: During a retrofit design review you must pick the backbone the new CMS will use to move streaming A/V between head-end and seatback displays.
practice.question: Which protocol do modern CMSs use for high-speed device-to-device transfer?
choices: Ethernet-based networking is the CMS high-speed backbone.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the primary function of an aircraft audio panel.

questions.targetSourceStem: What primary service does an aircraft audio panel provide to the flight crew?

flashcard.front: What core service does an aircraft audio panel deliver to the pilots?
flashcard.back: It selects and routes communication audio between installed radios and crew headset stations.

jeopardy.clue: This cockpit unit gives the flight crew selection and routing of communication audio among installed radios.

multipleChoice.stem: The primary role of the cockpit audio panel is to provide the flight crew with which capability?
choices: Selection and routing of communication audio across installed radios
Amplification of engine acoustic signatures into the flight deck
Generation of the emergency locator distress carrier
Interfacing with the cabin passenger entertainment system

practice.stem: A technician is briefing a new apprentice on cockpit avionics. Which statement best describes the primary service the audio panel provides to the flight crew?
choices: It handles selection and routing of communication audio across the installed radios.
It amplifies engine mechanical noise into the cockpit for crew monitoring.
It generates the distress carrier used by the emergency locator transmitter.
It acts as the interface to the cabin passenger entertainment equipment.

preassessment.stem: Which primary service does a cockpit audio panel provide to the flight crew during normal operation?
choices: Communication audio selection and routing among the installed radios
Cockpit amplification of engine compartment mechanical sounds
Generation of the emergency locator distress transmission

questions.learningObjective: Identify the audio panel feature that keeps intercom speech off the transmitted radio signal.
questions.fullObjective: Given an audio panel description, the technician shall identify the intercom-to-radio isolation circuit as the feature that prevents crew intercom conversation from being keyed onto the transmitted radio carrier.
questions.targetSourceStem: The audio panel contains circuits that prevent intercom conversation from being broadcast on the radio. What is this feature called?

flashcard.front: What audio panel feature stops crew intercom chatter from being keyed out on the transmitted radio?
flashcard.back: Intercom-to-radio isolation, which separates the intercom bus from the transmit audio path.

jeopardy.clue: This audio panel circuit keeps crew-to-crew intercom speech off the outgoing radio transmission.

multipleChoice.stem: An audio panel keeps crew intercom speech from being transmitted over the radio. This feature is known as:

practice.stem: During a systems briefing, a student asks what keeps the copilot's intercom remarks from being heard by ATC when the pilot transmits. The correct answer is that the audio panel provides which function?
choices: VHF interference filtering on the receiver front end only
Intercom-to-radio isolation within the transmit audio path
Transmit power amplification between the exciter and antenna
Standby power consumption reduction on the audio bus

preassessment.stem: What feature inside an audio panel keeps crew intercom conversation from being broadcast over the radio?

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose loss of transmit sidetone to the pilot's headset.
questions.fullObjective: Given a report of a pilot unable to hear their own voice while keying the radio, the technician shall identify the transmit sidetone circuit as the most likely faulty audio panel circuit.
questions.targetSourceStem: A pilot cannot hear their own voice when transmitting on the radio. What audio panel circuit is likely faulty?

flashcard.front: Which audio panel circuit is suspect when a pilot cannot hear himself while keying the transmit radio?
flashcard.back: The transmit sidetone circuit, which feeds mic audio back to the headset during transmit.

jeopardy.clue: When a pilot cannot hear their own voice on the headset during transmit, this audio panel circuit is almost always the cause.

multipleChoice.stem: A pilot reports hearing no self-voice feedback while keying the COM radio. Which audio panel circuit is most likely faulty?

practice.stem: After an audio panel replacement, the captain reports that mic audio appears absent in the headset whenever the PTT is pressed, though ATC can hear the transmission clearly. Which circuit is the technician's first suspect?
choices: Marker beacon decoding circuit that identifies inner and middle markers
Receiver squelch circuit that gates received audio during idle
Transmit sidetone circuit that feeds mic audio back to the headset
Music input muting circuit that silences entertainment on transmit

preassessment.stem: If a pilot cannot hear their own voice in the headset when keying the radio, which audio panel circuit is most likely defective?

questions.learningObjective: Describe what the squelch control sets on an audio panel.
questions.fullObjective: Given an audio panel with a squelch control, the technician shall describe that the control adjusts the noise threshold at which the receiver audio is muted.
questions.targetSourceStem: What does the squelch control on an audio panel adjust?

flashcard.front: What parameter does the squelch knob on a communication audio panel set?
flashcard.back: It sets the noise threshold below which receiver audio is muted from the headset.

jeopardy.clue: On a COM audio panel, this knob sets the signal-to-noise threshold that mutes receiver output until a valid carrier appears.

multipleChoice.stem: The squelch control on an audio panel is used to set:
multipleChoice.explanation: The squelch control sets the noise threshold below which the receiver audio is muted.

practice.stem: A pilot asks which adjustment the squelch knob on the audio panel actually affects. Which response is correct?
choices: It adjusts the audio level of the marker beacon tones only.
It adjusts the microphone sensitivity of the pilot's boom mic.
It adjusts the left-right balance heard in the pilot's headset.
It adjusts the noise threshold used to mute the receiver output.

preassessment.stem: What function is adjusted by the squelch control on an audio panel?
choices: Microphone input sensitivity for the boom mic
Receiver noise threshold for muting weak signals

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose unwanted copilot audio on the transmit signal.
questions.fullObjective: Given a symptom of the copilot's voice being broadcast over the radio while the pilot transmits, the technician shall identify failed intercom isolation within the audio panel as the fault.
questions.targetSourceStem: During a radio check, ATC hears the copilot's voice on the frequency even though only the pilot is transmitting. What is the fault?

flashcard.front: ATC hears the copilot's voice when the pilot transmits alone. Which audio panel fault is indicated?
flashcard.back: Failure of the intercom-to-radio isolation circuit so the intercom bus bleeds into transmit audio.

jeopardy.clue: When ATC receives the copilot's voice while the pilot alone is keying the radio, this internal audio panel fault is the cause.

multipleChoice.stem: ATC reports hearing the copilot's voice on the radio while only the pilot is keying the PTT. Which fault in the audio panel explains this symptom?
choices: Pilot PTT switch is stuck closed

practice.stem: During a ground radio check, ATC can hear the copilot chatting in the cockpit even though only the pilot is pressing the PTT. What is the most likely fault?
choices: The intercom isolation circuit in the audio panel has failed.
The antenna feedline has a severe impedance mismatch.
The pilot's PTT switch is mechanically stuck in the closed position.
The pilot's microphone element has developed an internal short.

preassessment.stem: ATC hears the copilot speaking on the radio when only the pilot is keying. The fault most likely lies in:
choices: Failure of the intercom-to-radio isolation circuit
Mismatched impedance at the COM antenna
A PTT switch stuck in the closed position
An internal short in the mic element

questions.learningObjective: Isolate a no-voice-carrier-present fault to the audio panel mic routing.
questions.fullObjective: Given that a radio keys a carrier but passes no voice, and a mic connected directly to the radio works, the technician shall isolate the fault to the audio panel microphone routing circuit.
questions.targetSourceStem: ATC hears carrier but no voice when the pilot transmits. A test microphone connected directly to the radio works normally. Where is the fault?

flashcard.front: Radio keys carrier with no voice, but a direct-connected test mic works. Where is the fault?
flashcard.back: Inside the audio panel's microphone routing circuit between the pilot station and the COM radio.

jeopardy.clue: When the radio shows carrier but no modulated voice, and bypassing this unit with a direct mic clears the fault, this is where the break lives.
choices: The audio panel mic routing circuit

multipleChoice.stem: ATC hears a keyed carrier but no voice from the pilot. A jack-plugged test microphone wired directly into the COM radio transmits normally. The fault is in:
choices: The COM radio transmitter final amplifier
The audio panel microphone routing circuit
The aircraft main battery charging bus

practice.stem: A pilot reports that ATC hears an open carrier but no speech when he transmits. The technician bypasses the installation by plugging a test mic directly into the COM radio and the transmission comes through clearly. Where is the fault?

Inside the audio panel's microphone routing circuit
Inside the COM radio's transmitter final amplifier
Inside the main battery charging regulator circuit

preassessment.stem: If ATC hears a keyed carrier but no voice, and a mic directly connected to the radio works, the defective element is:
choices: The battery charger regulator on the main bus
The mic routing circuit inside the audio panel
The coaxial connector at the COM antenna
The final amplifier inside the COM radio

questions.learningObjective: Predict the audio symptom of a low-impedance headset on a high-impedance output.

questions.targetSourceStem: A technician connects a low-impedance headset to an audio output designed for high-impedance headsets. What symptom will the pilot report?

flashcard.front: What symptom results when a low-impedance headset is plugged into a high-impedance audio output?
flashcard.back: Very low perceived volume even with the headset volume turned to its maximum setting.

jeopardy.clue: When a low-impedance headset is plugged into a jack expecting a high-impedance load, this is the symptom the pilot reports.
choices: Excessive volume that cannot be reduced
Very low volume even at maximum setting
Intermittent audio cutting in and out

multipleChoice.stem: A low-impedance headset is connected to an audio output designed for a high-impedance load. The pilot will most likely report:

Very low audio level at maximum volume setting
Loud squealing feedback oscillation in the headset

questions.learningObjective: Locate the fault for marker lights working without audio.

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose a hot mic condition without PTT press.

The audio panel volume is too high

That the COM radio transmit stage is not stuck in the on state
That the microphone element does not have an internal short
That the audio panel master volume is not adjusted too high

preassessment.stem: When the intercom mic transmits continuously without any PTT press, the most likely cause is:
choices: VOX voice activation mode is enabled

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose a 400 Hz hum in crew headsets after installation.
questions.fullObjective: Given a 400 Hz hum in the crew headsets after an audio panel installation, the technician shall identify a poor ground connection to aircraft structure as the most likely installation error.

A poor ground connection to aircraft structure
A ground wire bundled inside a power conduit
A ground wire routed to the battery post

A poor ground connection to aircraft structure

A ground wire with corroded ring terminal

A poor ground connection from the audio panel to aircraft structure

A ground wire with corroded lug hardware at the terminal strip

preassessment.stem: A 400 Hz hum appearing in every headset after installation is most commonly caused by:

questions.learningObjective: Describe the minimum capability of audio panel emergency mode.

flashcard.back: It provides a flashing annunciator combined with an audible warning tone.

jeopardy.clue: Modern audio panels use this combined visual and aural method to warn the crew of a stuck mic condition.
choices: An automatic reduction of antenna power
An emergency broadcast on guard frequency
A flashing indicator with a warning tone

Reducing antenna transmit power below licensed output

questions.learningObjective: Explain how DME computes slant range to the ground station.

flashcard.front: How does DME determine the slant range to the ground station?
flashcard.back: It times the round-trip delay of its interrogation pulse and the ground transponder's reply pulse.

jeopardy.clue: DME computes slant range using this timing measurement between the airborne interrogator and the ground transponder.

multipleChoice.stem: DME calculates distance to the ground station by:
choices: Detecting the Doppler frequency shift of the carrier
Measuring the amplitude reduction of the returned signal
Comparing phase angles of two received signals
Timing the round-trip delay of its interrogation pulses

The circuits compare the phase difference between two transmitted signals.
The set detects the Doppler frequency shift produced by aircraft motion.
The interrogator times the round-trip delay of its pulse and the ground reply.

preassessment.stem: DME determines distance to the ground station by:
preassessment.explanation: DME measures slant range by timing the round-trip delay of interrogation pulses sent to the ground transponder.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the regulatory source for altimeter test procedures and tolerances.
questions.fullObjective: Given an altimeter inspection requirement, identify the Federal Aviation Regulation appendix that contains the detailed test procedures and tolerances used to certify altimeters for return to service.
questions.targetSourceStem: What document contains the detailed test procedures and tolerances for altimeter inspections?

flashcard.front: Which regulatory appendix specifies the test procedures and tolerances for altimeter inspections?
flashcard.back: 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix E contains the altimeter system and altitude reporting equipment inspection procedures and tolerances.
flashcard.explanation: Appendix E to Part 43 lists scale error, hysteresis, after-effect, friction, and case leak tolerances at specific altitudes. Appendix F covers ATC transponder tests, and AC 43.13-1B is advisory only.

jeopardy.clue: This Part 43 appendix prescribes the test procedures and tolerances used when inspecting aircraft altimeters.
jeopardy.explanation: Appendix E to Part 43 is the authoritative source for altimeter test procedures and tolerances. Appendix F covers transponder tests.

multipleChoice.stem: A technician is preparing to perform an altimeter inspection and needs the authoritative test procedures and tolerances. Which document must be used?
multipleChoice.explanation: Appendix E to Part 43 is the regulatory source for altimeter test procedures and tolerances. Appendix F is for transponders, and AC 43.13-1B is advisory.

practice.stem: During an altimeter inspection, the technician must refer to the regulatory document containing the required test procedures and tolerances. Which reference provides this data?
choices: The aircraft type certificate data sheet
practice.explanation: Appendix E to Part 43 contains altimeter test procedures, tolerances, and the required static system leak check values.

preassessment.stem: Which regulatory document contains the altimeter inspection test procedures and tolerances?
preassessment.explanation: Appendix E to Part 43 is the regulatory source for altimeter inspection procedures and tolerances.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the certificate required for a facility to perform pitot-static certification tests.

questions.targetSourceStem: What authorization is required for a facility to perform pitot-static system certification tests?

flashcard.front: What facility authorization is required to perform pitot-static system certification tests?
flashcard.back: A Part 145 repair station certificate with an appropriate instrument class rating is required.
flashcard.explanation: An individual A&P cannot sign off a pitot-static certification unless operating under a certificated repair station with the correct rating. The test is an approval for return to service event.

jeopardy.clue: A shop needs this type of FAA certificate, with an instrument class rating, to legally perform pitot-static certification tests.
jeopardy.explanation: Part 145 is the repair station rule, and the shop must hold an instrument class rating covering altimeters and static systems.

multipleChoice.stem: A shop wants to perform and sign off pitot-static system certification tests. What facility-level authorization must the shop hold?
choices: Any A&P mechanic certificate without additional restrictions
A Part 145 repair station certificate with an appropriate instrument rating
multipleChoice.explanation: Pitot-static certification must be performed by a Part 145 repair station rated for the class of work or by the aircraft manufacturer.

practice.stem: Which facility-level certificate must a shop hold to perform pitot-static system certification tests on a customer aircraft?
choices: A Part 145 repair station certificate with an appropriate rating
An A&P mechanic certificate held by a single technician
practice.explanation: Only a Part 145 repair station rated for the class of work, or the manufacturer, may perform and return a pitot-static certification to service.

preassessment.stem: What certificate must a facility hold to perform pitot-static system certification tests?
choices: A Part 145 station with proper rating
preassessment.explanation: A Part 145 repair station holding the appropriate rating is required to return pitot-static certification to service.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the regulation that defines maximum pitot-static leak rates.
questions.fullObjective: Given a pitot-static leak-rate question, identify the Federal Aviation Regulation that establishes the maximum allowable leak rate for an aircraft's pitot-static system.
questions.targetSourceStem: Which Federal Aviation Regulation defines the maximum allowable leak rate for aircraft pitot-static systems?

flashcard.front: Which Federal Aviation Regulation defines the maximum allowable leak rate for a pitot-static system?
flashcard.back: 14 CFR 23.1325 (small airplanes) or 25.1325 (transport airplanes) establish the type-certification leak rate limits.
flashcard.explanation: The airworthiness standards in Parts 23 and 25 set the static system leak limits. 91.411 requires the test; 91.413 covers transponders.

jeopardy.clue: These Parts 23 and 25 sections establish the maximum allowable leak rate for aircraft pitot-static systems.
jeopardy.explanation: The airworthiness standards in 23.1325 and 25.1325 define static system leak limits at the type-certification level.

multipleChoice.stem: Which Federal Aviation Regulation defines the maximum allowable leak rate for aircraft pitot-static systems?
choices: 14 CFR 91.411, which schedules the periodic tests
14 CFR 23.1325 or 25.1325, depending on type certification
multipleChoice.explanation: Parts 23 and 25 airworthiness standards establish the leak rate. 91.411 requires the test and 91.413 covers transponders.

practice.stem: When questioned about the maximum allowable static-system leak rate, which regulation should the technician cite as establishing that limit?
practice.explanation: Parts 23 and 25 set the leak-rate airworthiness limits (depending on type certification). 91.411 schedules the check but does not set the rate; 91.413 covers transponders.

preassessment.stem: Which regulation defines the maximum allowable leak rate for aircraft pitot-static systems?
preassessment.explanation: 14 CFR 23.1325 (and 25.1325 for transport-category aircraft) is the airworthiness section that sets the allowable leak rate. 91.411 requires the check but does not set the rate.

questions.learningObjective: State the maximum allowable static-system leak rate for a pressurized aircraft.
questions.fullObjective: Given a pressurized aircraft undergoing static-system testing, state the maximum allowable leak rate expressed in both percentage and feet terms.
questions.targetSourceStem: What is the maximum allowable static system leak rate for a pressurized aircraft?

flashcard.front: What is the maximum allowable static-system leak rate for a pressurized aircraft?
flashcard.back: 2% of the test pressure or 100 feet per minute, whichever is greater.
flashcard.explanation: Per Part 23/25 airworthiness data and Part 43 Appendix E, the pressurized aircraft limit is expressed as 2% or 100 feet, whichever is greater, tested over one minute.

jeopardy.clue: The maximum allowable static-system leak rate for a pressurized aircraft is expressed as 2% or this figure, whichever is greater.
choices: 50 feet per minute sustained for five minutes
Zero leak with no tolerance permitted
100 feet per minute measured at 18,000 feet
jeopardy.explanation: The regulatory leak allowance for pressurized aircraft is 2% of the test pressure or 100 feet, whichever is greater.

multipleChoice.stem: The technician is checking static-system leakage on a pressurized aircraft. What leak allowance may the system exhibit and remain airworthy?
choices: A constant 100 feet per minute when held at 18,000 feet
A constant 50 feet per minute for five minutes
Zero leak, with no tolerance allowed
2% of the test pressure or 100 feet, whichever is greater
multipleChoice.explanation: For pressurized aircraft the static-system leak rate must not exceed 2% of the test pressure or 100 feet, whichever is greater.

practice.stem: During a static-leak check on a pressurized aircraft, what is the maximum allowable leak rate before the aircraft is unairworthy?
choices: A steady 100 feet per minute above 18,000 feet
A steady 50 feet per minute for a five-minute test
Zero leak with no allowable tolerance
2% of the test pressure or 100 feet, whichever is greater
practice.explanation: Pressurized aircraft allow 2% of test pressure or 100 feet, whichever is greater, over a one-minute test.

preassessment.stem: What is the maximum allowable static-system leak rate for a pressurized aircraft?
choices: 50 feet per minute over five minutes
Zero leak permitted at any altitude
2% of test pressure or 100 ft, greater
preassessment.explanation: The regulatory leak limit is 2% of test pressure or 100 feet, whichever is greater.

questions.learningObjective: State the test altitude used for the altimeter case leak check.
questions.fullObjective: Given the altimeter case leak test procedure in Part 43 Appendix E, identify the indicated altitude at which the case leak check is conducted.
questions.targetSourceStem: At what indicated altitude is the altimeter case leak test performed?

flashcard.front: At what indicated altitude is the altimeter case leak test performed?
flashcard.back: 18,000 feet MSL, following the procedure in Part 43 Appendix E.
flashcard.explanation: The case leak is checked by holding the altimeter indication at 18,000 feet MSL for one minute and verifying the drift stays within tolerance.

jeopardy.clue: The altimeter case leak test is performed at this indicated altitude on the MSL scale.
jeopardy.explanation: Appendix E specifies an 18,000 ft MSL hold for the case leak check. AGL is never referenced in altimeter testing.

multipleChoice.stem: Per Part 43 Appendix E, at what indicated altitude is the altimeter case leak test conducted?
multipleChoice.explanation: The procedure prescribes a case leak check at 18,000 feet MSL. AGL is not used in certification testing.
