practice.stem: A technician is conducting the altimeter case leak check. At what test altitude is this check performed?
practice.explanation: Appendix E prescribes 18,000 feet MSL as the altitude for the case leak check.

preassessment.stem: At what indicated altitude is the altimeter case leak test conducted?
preassessment.explanation: The case leak check is performed at 18,000 feet MSL.

questions.learningObjective: State the maximum allowable altimeter case leak rate.
questions.fullObjective: Given a completed altimeter case leak test, state the maximum allowable altitude drift rate per minute that keeps the altimeter within tolerance.
questions.targetSourceStem: What is the maximum allowable leak rate for an altimeter case leak test?

flashcard.front: What is the maximum allowable leak rate for an altimeter case leak test?
flashcard.back: Plus or minus 100 feet per minute at 18,000 feet indicated altitude.
flashcard.explanation: Appendix E to Part 43 limits altimeter case leakage to 100 feet per minute in either direction during the one-minute hold.

jeopardy.clue: This is the maximum allowable per-minute drift during an altimeter case leak test.
choices: Plus or minus 10 feet per minute
Plus or minus 100 feet per minute
A positive 100 feet per minute only
Zero leak permitted at any altitude
jeopardy.explanation: The allowed case leak drift is 100 feet per minute in either direction during the one-minute test at 18,000 feet.

multipleChoice.stem: After stabilizing at the case leak test altitude, what is the maximum acceptable drift rate during the one-minute hold?
choices: Plus or minus 10 feet per minute
Plus or minus 100 feet per minute
100 feet per minute in the positive direction only
Zero leak with no allowable tolerance
multipleChoice.explanation: Appendix E limits altimeter case leakage to 100 fpm in either direction.

practice.stem: The altimeter case leak test is conducted and the case pressure drifts the indicator downward. What maximum rate is acceptable?
choices: Plus or minus 10 feet per minute
Plus or minus 100 feet per minute
100 feet per minute upward drift only
Zero leak with no allowable tolerance
practice.explanation: Appendix E permits 100 fpm of drift in either direction during the case leak test.

preassessment.stem: What is the maximum allowable leak rate during an altimeter case leak test?
choices: 100 feet per minute in one direction
Plus or minus 100 feet per minute
Plus or minus 10 feet per minute
preassessment.explanation: Case leak tolerance is 100 fpm in either direction.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the source of RVSM inspection test procedures and tolerances.
questions.fullObjective: Given an RVSM-certified aircraft undergoing an RVSM inspection, identify the document that specifies the procedures and tolerances applied during that inspection.
questions.targetSourceStem: When performing an RVSM inspection, which document specifies the test procedures and tolerances?

flashcard.front: Which document specifies the test procedures and tolerances for an RVSM inspection?
flashcard.back: The OEM or STC holder's RVSM maintenance data package approved by the FAA.
flashcard.explanation: RVSM tolerances are aircraft-specific. They appear in the type certificate holder's or STC holder's RVSM MEL and maintenance manual, not in 91.411 or 91.413.

jeopardy.clue: This is the authoritative source for the test procedures and tolerances used during an RVSM inspection.
choices: Aircraft manufacturer data alone with no STC
OEM or STC holder data package
jeopardy.explanation: RVSM procedures and tolerances live in the OEM or STC holder FAA-approved data package for that aircraft.

multipleChoice.stem: A technician is scheduling an RVSM inspection. Which document specifies the test procedures and tolerances to be applied?
choices: The aircraft manufacturer structural repair manual alone
The OEM or STC holder data package for RVSM maintenance
multipleChoice.explanation: RVSM tolerances are tailored to the aircraft and live in the OEM or STC holder approved data package.

practice.stem: Where does the technician find the RVSM inspection test procedures and the tolerances required for that aircraft?
choices: Only the airframe manufacturer structural repair manual
The OEM or STC holder RVSM maintenance data package
practice.explanation: The OEM or STC holder's FAA-approved RVSM maintenance data package provides the procedures and tolerances.

preassessment.stem: Which document specifies RVSM inspection test procedures and tolerances?
choices: The OEM or STC holder data package
preassessment.explanation: RVSM tolerances are aircraft-specific and reside in the OEM or STC data package.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the tolerance standard applied to a standby altimeter in RVSM aircraft.
questions.fullObjective: Given an RVSM-certified aircraft, identify which test tolerance (RVSM or standard) is applied to the standby altimeter during inspection.
questions.targetSourceStem: During RVSM inspection, which standard is used to test the standby altimeter?

flashcard.front: During an RVSM inspection, which tolerance standard is used to test the standby altimeter?
flashcard.back: Standard 91.411 tolerances only; RVSM tolerances apply only to the primary altimeters used for altitude hold.
flashcard.explanation: The standby is not used to maintain altitude in RVSM airspace, so it is tested to 91.411 tolerances rather than the tighter RVSM limits.

jeopardy.clue: Only this standard's tolerances apply to the standby altimeter during an RVSM inspection.
choices: The same RVSM tolerances as the primary altimeters
No testing is required on the standby
jeopardy.explanation: The standby altimeter is tested to the basic 91.411 tolerances because it is not the altitude-hold source.

multipleChoice.stem: When inspecting an RVSM-certified aircraft, which tolerance standard must be applied to the standby altimeter?
choices: The same RVSM tolerances applied to the primary altimeters
No testing is required on the standby altimeter
multipleChoice.explanation: Standby altimeters are tested to basic 91.411 tolerances, not tighter RVSM limits.

practice.stem: A technician tests the standby altimeter on an RVSM-certified aircraft. Which tolerance standard applies to that altimeter?
choices: No testing is required for the standby altimeter
The same RVSM tolerances used on the primary altimeters
practice.explanation: The standby is not used for altitude-hold in RVSM, so it is tested to 91.411 tolerances only.

preassessment.stem: During an RVSM inspection, which tolerance is applied to the standby altimeter?
choices: Same as the primary RVSM tolerances
preassessment.explanation: The standby altimeter is tested to 91.411 tolerances only.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the flight level range defining RVSM airspace.
questions.fullObjective: Given a question about 1,000-foot vertical separation, identify the flight level band within which RVSM operations are authorized.
questions.targetSourceStem: What flight level range defines RVSM airspace where 1000-foot separation is used?

flashcard.front: What flight level range defines RVSM airspace in which 1,000-foot separation is applied?
flashcard.back: FL290 through FL410, inclusive.
flashcard.explanation: Reduced Vertical Separation Minimums apply in the band from FL290 to FL410, enabling 1,000 ft separation between aircraft.

jeopardy.clue: This flight level range defines RVSM airspace where 1,000-foot vertical separation applies.
jeopardy.explanation: RVSM operations are authorized between FL290 and FL410, inclusive.

multipleChoice.stem: Within which flight level range does RVSM airspace permit 1,000-foot vertical separation?
multipleChoice.explanation: RVSM airspace is defined as FL290 to FL410 inclusive.

practice.stem: An aircraft plans to take advantage of 1,000-foot separation under RVSM. Which flight level range defines that airspace?
practice.explanation: RVSM applies from FL290 to FL410, inclusive.

preassessment.stem: What is the RVSM flight level range?
preassessment.explanation: RVSM airspace is FL290 to FL410.

questions.learningObjective: State the maximum total vertical error allowed in RVSM airspace.
questions.fullObjective: Given an RVSM-certified aircraft, identify the maximum total vertical error the aircraft must maintain to remain authorized for RVSM operation.
questions.targetSourceStem: What is the maximum total vertical error allowed for an aircraft operating in RVSM airspace?

flashcard.front: What is the maximum total vertical error (TVE) allowed for an aircraft in RVSM airspace?
flashcard.back: Plus or minus 245 feet.
flashcard.explanation: RVSM-approved aircraft must maintain TVE within +/-245 ft. Exceeding that triggers a system inspection before returning to RVSM.

jeopardy.clue: This is the maximum total vertical error allowed for an aircraft operating in RVSM airspace.
choices: Only what the maintenance manual says
jeopardy.explanation: RVSM tolerance for total vertical error is +/-245 ft.

multipleChoice.stem: An RVSM-approved aircraft must maintain total vertical error within what limit?
choices: There is no specific regulatory requirement
multipleChoice.explanation: Total vertical error may not exceed +/-245 feet in RVSM airspace.

practice.stem: During RVSM monitoring, what maximum total vertical error must an aircraft not exceed?
choices: Whatever the aircraft's maintenance manual allows
There is no specific regulatory requirement
practice.explanation: RVSM TVE tolerance is +/-245 feet.

preassessment.stem: What is the maximum total vertical error allowed in RVSM airspace?
preassessment.explanation: RVSM TVE tolerance is +/-245 feet.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the authorization required before operating an RVSM-equipped aircraft in RVSM airspace.
questions.fullObjective: Given a properly equipped and tested RVSM aircraft, identify the additional FAA authorization required before the operator may fly in RVSM airspace.
questions.targetSourceStem: An aircraft owner wants to operate in RVSM airspace. The required equipment is installed and passes testing. What additional authorization is required?

flashcard.front: What additional authorization is required to operate an RVSM-equipped aircraft in RVSM airspace?
flashcard.back: An aircraft-specific RVSM Letter of Authorization (LOA) issued by the FAA, or equivalent automatic authorization program enrollment.
flashcard.explanation: Equipment and testing alone are not sufficient; the operator needs an FAA LOA, and for part 91 aircraft with ADS-B Out compliance the current automatic authorization may apply.

jeopardy.clue: This FAA-issued document specifically authorizes an operator to fly an equipped and tested aircraft in RVSM airspace.
choices: None, if the equipment passes testing
jeopardy.explanation: An aircraft-specific RVSM LOA is required in addition to equipment and testing (or the automatic ADS-B Out-based authorization pathway).

multipleChoice.stem: An owner has installed and successfully tested the required RVSM equipment. What additional FAA authorization must be obtained before RVSM operations?
choices: A pilot RVSM training certificate alone
No authorization if the equipment passes testing
An aircraft-specific RVSM Letter of Authorization
multipleChoice.explanation: An aircraft-specific RVSM LOA is required unless the operator qualifies for the automatic ADS-B Out-based authorization.

practice.stem: After passing all RVSM equipment tests, what additional FAA authorization is required before the aircraft may fly in RVSM airspace?
choices: A single logbook endorsement with no expiration
No authorization beyond the passing test data
An aircraft-specific RVSM Letter of Authorization
practice.explanation: An RVSM LOA, tied to the aircraft and operator, is required (or equivalent automatic ADS-B Out authorization).

preassessment.stem: What additional authorization is required to fly an equipped and tested aircraft in RVSM airspace?
choices: None if the equipment passes checks
preassessment.explanation: An aircraft-specific RVSM Letter of Authorization is required for operation in RVSM airspace.

questions.learningObjective: State the maintenance action required after an RVSM altitude deviation.
questions.fullObjective: Given a reported RVSM altitude deviation, identify the required corrective action before the aircraft is returned to RVSM operations.
questions.targetSourceStem: A pilot reports an altitude deviation while operating in RVSM airspace. What is required before returning to RVSM operations?

flashcard.front: What maintenance action is required after an RVSM altitude deviation before returning to RVSM operations?
flashcard.back: A system inspection performed in accordance with the approved RVSM maintenance data.
flashcard.explanation: An altitude deviation invalidates RVSM airworthiness until a system inspection per approved data confirms the altimetry system is within tolerance again.

jeopardy.clue: This is required after an RVSM altitude deviation before the aircraft may return to RVSM operations.
choices: A logbook entry of the pilot report only
Replacement of every altimeter on the aircraft
jeopardy.explanation: An RVSM altitude deviation triggers a system inspection in accordance with the approved RVSM maintenance data.

multipleChoice.stem: Following a reported RVSM altitude deviation, what maintenance action must be completed before the aircraft returns to RVSM operations?
choices: Replacement of all altimeters on the aircraft
A logbook entry documenting the pilot report only
A system inspection performed per the approved RVSM data
multipleChoice.explanation: An altitude deviation requires an inspection per the OEM or STC holder RVSM data before returning the aircraft to RVSM ops.

practice.stem: A crew reports an RVSM altitude deviation. What must the technician perform before the aircraft is returned to RVSM service?
choices: A logbook entry summarizing the pilot's verbal report only
A system inspection in accordance with approved RVSM data
practice.explanation: An altitude deviation mandates an RVSM system inspection per the approved maintenance data before RVSM service resumes.

preassessment.stem: After an RVSM altitude deviation, what must be done before returning the aircraft to RVSM service?
choices: Logbook entry of the pilot report only
System inspection per approved RVSM data
preassessment.explanation: A system inspection per approved RVSM data is required.

questions.learningObjective: State the interval for RVSM certification testing.
questions.fullObjective: Given an RVSM-certified aircraft, identify the source and typical interval for recurring RVSM certification testing.
questions.targetSourceStem: How frequently must RVSM certification testing be performed?

flashcard.front: How frequently must RVSM certification testing be performed?
flashcard.back: Per the OEM or STC holder RVSM maintenance data; the interval is typically 12 or 24 months.
flashcard.explanation: The aircraft-specific RVSM data package sets the interval, which may be 12 or 24 months depending on the design. 91.411 alone does not govern RVSM testing.

jeopardy.clue: This determines the recurring RVSM certification interval, which is typically 12 or 24 months.
choices: OEM or STC holder data, typically 12 or 24 months
Every 12 months with no exceptions allowed
No specific recurring interval is required
jeopardy.explanation: RVSM recurring intervals are set by the OEM or STC holder maintenance data package, typically 12 or 24 months.

multipleChoice.stem: What governs the recurring interval for RVSM certification testing?
choices: OEM or STC holder data, typically 12 or 24 months
Only 91.411 at 24 months, with no exceptions
Only a 12-month interval with no exceptions
No specific recurring interval is specified
multipleChoice.explanation: The RVSM interval is set by the approved OEM or STC data; most aircraft fall into 12 or 24 month cycles.

practice.stem: A technician must schedule recurring RVSM testing. Which source and interval are correct?
choices: OEM or STC data, usually 12 or 24 months
Exclusively every 12 months in every case
No recurring interval is ever specified
practice.explanation: The recurring RVSM interval comes from the OEM or STC holder's data, typically 12 or 24 months. 91.411 does not set the RVSM recheck cycle.

preassessment.stem: How often must RVSM certification testing be performed?
preassessment.explanation: OEM or STC data sets the recurring RVSM interval, which is typically 12 or 24 months; 91.411 does not govern RVSM rechecks.

questions.learningObjective: Compare RVSM altimeter tolerances with standard 91.411 tolerances.
questions.fullObjective: Given both RVSM and standard 91.411 tolerance sets, identify which imposes tighter altimeter performance limits.
questions.targetSourceStem: How do RVSM altimeter tolerances compare to standard 91.411 tolerances?

flashcard.front: How do RVSM altimeter tolerances compare to standard 91.411 tolerances?
flashcard.back: RVSM tolerances are tighter than the standard 91.411 tolerances because 1,000-foot separation demands more precise altitude-keeping.
flashcard.explanation: RVSM was created on the basis that certified systems could maintain tighter altitude error than basic 91.411 systems, enabling 1,000 ft separation at altitude.

jeopardy.clue: This is how RVSM altimeter tolerances compare to standard 91.411 tolerances.
choices: RVSM only tests the transponder, not the altimeter
jeopardy.explanation: RVSM performance tolerances are tighter than 91.411 because 1,000-foot separation requires improved altitude-keeping.

multipleChoice.stem: How do RVSM altimeter performance tolerances compare to standard 91.411 tolerances?
choices: RVSM tests only the transponder, not the altimeter
RVSM tolerances are tighter than 91.411 tolerances
The 91.411 tolerances are more stringent than RVSM
The RVSM and 91.411 tolerance sets are identical
multipleChoice.explanation: RVSM imposes tighter tolerances than 91.411 to preserve 1,000 ft separation.

practice.stem: A technician is asked whether RVSM tolerances are the same as 91.411 tolerances. What is the correct response?
choices: RVSM tolerances are tighter than 91.411 tolerances
practice.explanation: RVSM requires tighter altitude-keeping than basic 91.411.

preassessment.stem: How do RVSM altimeter tolerances compare to 91.411 tolerances?
preassessment.explanation: RVSM tolerances are tighter than 91.411.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the physical property governed by the AWG number.
questions.fullObjective: Given a wire gauge specification, identify the conductor physical property that the American Wire Gauge number represents.
questions.targetSourceStem: The American Wire Gauge system assigns numbers to wire sizes. What physical property determines the AWG number?

flashcard.front: What physical property of a conductor does the American Wire Gauge number represent?
flashcard.back: The conductor's cross-sectional area (circular-mil area).
flashcard.explanation: AWG is a dimensional standard tied to circular-mil cross-sectional area. Current capacity and resistance are consequences of the area, insulation, and length, not the defining property.

jeopardy.clue: This conductor property is what the American Wire Gauge number directly quantifies.
jeopardy.explanation: AWG is a dimensional standard that specifies the conductor's cross-sectional area.

multipleChoice.stem: Which conductor physical property is used to assign a specific American Wire Gauge number to a wire?
choices: The cross-sectional area of the conductor
multipleChoice.explanation: AWG is defined by the conductor cross-sectional area, measured in circular mils.

practice.stem: A technician selects wire by AWG number. Which physical property does the AWG number represent?
choices: Type of metal used in the conductor
DC resistance per foot of the conductor
practice.explanation: AWG classifies wires by cross-sectional area. Ampacity and resistance are consequences of that area.

preassessment.stem: What property of a wire does the AWG number represent?
preassessment.explanation: AWG designates the conductor's cross-sectional area; current capacity and resistance per foot are consequences of that area, not the defining quantity.

questions.learningObjective: Select the correct stripping tool for insulation removal inside a connector backshell.
questions.fullObjective: Given a wire preparation task inside a crowded connector backshell, select the stripping tool that provides the most precise and damage-free insulation removal.
questions.targetSourceStem: A technician needs to strip insulation for a termination inside a crowded connector backshell. Which tool provides the best precision?

flashcard.front: Which stripping tool provides the best precision when stripping insulation inside a crowded connector backshell?
flashcard.back: A calibrated manual stripper with the correct die for the wire's gauge and insulation.
flashcard.explanation: Calibrated dies match AWG and insulation thickness, removing insulation cleanly without nicking strands. Self-adjusting and pliers-type strippers lack that precision for connector work.

jeopardy.clue: This stripping tool, matched with the correct dies, gives the cleanest insulation removal near a connector backshell.
choices: Calibrated manual stripper with correct dies
Electrician's pliers with a stripper notch
Razor blade rotated around the insulation
jeopardy.explanation: A calibrated manual stripper with gauge-specific dies gives the cleanest removal and protects the conductor strands.

multipleChoice.stem: Working within a crowded connector backshell, which stripping tool gives the best precision for insulation removal?
choices: A razor blade rotated around the insulation
A calibrated manual stripper with correct dies
Electrician's pliers with a stripper notch
multipleChoice.explanation: A calibrated manual stripper with matched dies cuts cleanly without nicking strands and fits in tight spaces.

practice.stem: A technician must strip tight-tolerance wires inside a MIL-DTL-38999 backshell. Which stripping tool is the best choice?
choices: A calibrated manual stripper with correct dies
Electrician's pliers with a stripper notch
A razor blade rotated around the wire
practice.explanation: Calibrated dies matched to the wire deliver repeatable, clean strips without damaging strands.

preassessment.stem: Which stripping tool is best for a backshell termination?
preassessment.explanation: Calibrated manual strippers with dies matched to the wire gauge are the approved tool; notched pliers, self-adjusting strippers, and razor blades damage conductors.

questions.learningObjective: Prioritize a flight-control interference check before securing a wire bundle.
questions.fullObjective: Given a new wire bundle routed near flight controls, identify the most critical pre-release verification to prevent interference.
questions.targetSourceStem: Before securing a new wire bundle near flight controls, what is the most critical verification?

flashcard.front: Before securing a wire bundle near flight controls, what is the most critical verification?
flashcard.back: Cycle the controls through their full range of travel and confirm the bundle never contacts moving surfaces.
flashcard.explanation: Clamp hardware, 1/2-inch spacing, and drip loops are all important, but none replace the regulatory requirement to prove no interference across the full control travel.

jeopardy.clue: This full-range verification is the most critical check before releasing a new wire bundle near flight controls.
choices: The bundle has a drip loop formed before clamping
Clamp part numbers match the maintenance manual
Controls move full range without bundle contact
The bundle sits 1/2 inch from any control cable
jeopardy.explanation: Proving no contact throughout the full range of motion is the regulatory requirement and catches clearance issues other checks miss.

multipleChoice.stem: Before final clamping of a new wire bundle routed near flight controls, which verification is most critical?
choices: Confirm clamps match the manufacturer part number
Maintain a 1/2-inch bundle offset from control cables
Cycle controls through full range with no bundle contact
Install a drip loop at the lowest point of the bundle
multipleChoice.explanation: No-interference through full control travel is the regulatory acceptance criterion and must be proved before release.

practice.stem: A technician has routed a new bundle next to elevator control cables. What is the most critical check before the install is released?
choices: Measure a 1/2-inch offset from each control cable
Verify clamp part numbers match the manual
Cycle the controls full range with no bundle contact
Install a drip loop at the lowest run
practice.explanation: Full-range control actuation without bundle contact proves that no binding or chafing will occur in service.

preassessment.stem: What is the most critical verification before securing a bundle near flight controls?
preassessment.explanation: Verify that flight controls move through full travel without contacting the wire bundle.

questions.learningObjective: State the maximum acceptable bonding resistance value.
questions.fullObjective: Given a bonding-jumper installation and a milliohm resistance reading, state the maximum acceptable resistance between a component and aircraft structure.
questions.targetSourceStem: After installing a bonding jumper, the technician measures resistance from the component to aircraft structure. What is the maximum acceptable reading?

flashcard.front: What is the maximum acceptable DC bonding resistance from a component to aircraft structure?
flashcard.back: 0.003 ohm (3 milliohms).
flashcard.explanation: Industry practice and AC 43.13-1B specify bonding resistance of no more than 3 milliohms (0.003 ohm) to minimize voltage drops and lightning currents.

jeopardy.clue: This is the maximum acceptable DC resistance from a bonded component to airframe structure.
jeopardy.explanation: Bonding resistance must be 0.003 ohm (3 milliohms) or less per accepted industry practice.

multipleChoice.stem: After bonding a component to structure, what is the maximum DC resistance measurement allowed?
multipleChoice.explanation: Bonding resistance must be 0.003 ohm or less.

practice.stem: A tech bonds an LRU tray and reads 0.004 ohm to structure. What is the maximum resistance the reading should be under?
practice.explanation: Accepted DC bonding resistance is 3 milliohms (0.003 ohm); the 0.004-ohm reading is out of limits.

preassessment.stem: What is the maximum acceptable DC bonding resistance?
preassessment.explanation: Bonding must be 3 milliohms or less.

questions.learningObjective: Explain why a bonding jumper is required when LRU mounts are non-conductive.
questions.fullObjective: Given an LRU tray mounted on non-conductive rubber shock mounts, explain the electrical purpose served by the bonding jumper to structure.
questions.targetSourceStem: An LRU tray is mounted on non-conductive rubber shock mounts. Why is a bonding jumper required between the tray and airframe?

flashcard.front: Why is a bonding jumper required when an LRU tray uses non-conductive rubber shock mounts?
flashcard.back: The jumper completes the electrical ground path from the LRU chassis to the airframe.
flashcard.explanation: Rubber mounts electrically isolate the tray, so without a jumper the chassis has no ground reference and EMI/fault currents have no return path.

jeopardy.clue: This is the electrical purpose of a bonding jumper between an LRU tray on rubber shock mounts and the airframe.
choices: Serves as a backup mechanical attachment
Prevents rubber shock mounts from drying out
jeopardy.explanation: Rubber shock mounts isolate the tray electrically, so the bonding jumper is needed to complete the ground path.

multipleChoice.stem: Rubber shock mounts isolate an LRU tray electrically. What purpose does the bonding jumper to the airframe serve?
choices: It completes the chassis ground path to the airframe
It provides a backup mechanical attachment
It prevents the shock mounts from drying out
multipleChoice.explanation: Rubber mounts block the ground path; the jumper provides the required DC/RF reference to structure.

practice.stem: An LRU tray is installed on rubber shock mounts. Why is a bonding jumper to the airframe required?
choices: It provides the electrical ground path
It dampens vibration beyond shock mounts
It backs up the mechanical attachment
It prevents rubber mounts from cracking
practice.explanation: The bonding jumper completes the electrical path from chassis to structure, bypassing the insulating rubber shock mounts.

preassessment.stem: Why is a bonding jumper needed when an LRU tray is on rubber shock mounts?
choices: To complete the electrical ground path
To back up the mechanical mount
preassessment.explanation: It completes the chassis ground path.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the hazard created when hot-stamping Kapton-insulated wire.
questions.fullObjective: Given a drawing prohibiting hot-stamp marking on Kapton-insulated wire, identify the failure mode the prohibition is intended to prevent.
questions.targetSourceStem: The engineering drawing prohibits hot-stamp marking on Kapton insulated wire. What hazard does hot-stamping create on this material?

flashcard.front: What hazard does hot-stamp marking create on Kapton-insulated wire?
flashcard.back: Micro-cracks in the polyimide insulation that can later propagate into arc tracking.
flashcard.explanation: Kapton polyimide is susceptible to arc tracking along micro-cracks. Drawings prohibit hot-stamping, relying on laser or ink-jet methods instead.

jeopardy.clue: This is the arc-tracking precursor created when hot-stamp marking is applied to Kapton insulation.
choices: Discoloration that hides the wire ID
jeopardy.explanation: Hot-stamping creates micro-cracks in polyimide that can trigger arc tracking under sustained voltage.

multipleChoice.stem: Why is hot-stamp marking prohibited on Kapton polyimide-insulated wire?
choices: It causes the marking to lose adhesion over time
It creates micro-cracks that can lead to arc tracking
It causes discoloration that hides wire identification
It anneals the conductor and reduces ampacity
multipleChoice.explanation: Micro-cracks in the Kapton jacket can initiate catastrophic arc tracking.

practice.stem: A drawing forbids hot-stamp marking on Kapton wire. What failure mode does this rule prevent?
choices: Micro-cracks that can initiate arc tracking
Adhesive failure of the printed marking
practice.explanation: The concern is micro-cracks leading to arc tracking, a known Kapton failure mode.

preassessment.stem: What hazard results from hot-stamping Kapton wire?
preassessment.explanation: Aged insulation develops micro-cracks that can initiate arc tracking, the principal safety-of-flight hazard.

questions.learningObjective: Select the correct wire type for COM-radio audio signals.
questions.fullObjective: Given a COM-radio audio installation, select the wire type that best prevents hum and noise pickup on microphone and headphone lines.
questions.targetSourceStem: What type of wire should be used for audio signals in a COM radio installation to prevent hum and noise?

flashcard.front: What wire type should be used for COM-radio audio signals to prevent hum and noise?
flashcard.back: Shielded audio cable, with the shield grounded at a single point per drawing.
flashcard.explanation: Shielded audio cable rejects electric-field coupling that causes hum. Coax is used for RF, not audio. Unshielded pairs are vulnerable to aircraft EMI sources.

jeopardy.clue: This wire type should be used for COM-radio audio signals to keep hum and noise out.
jeopardy.explanation: Shielded audio cable is standard for audio runs to reject EMI/RFI pickup.

multipleChoice.stem: Which wire type is appropriate for routing COM-radio audio signals to minimize hum and noise?
multipleChoice.explanation: Shielded audio cable rejects EMI and capacitive hum coupling.

practice.stem: A technician is installing new headset jacks in the panel. Which wire type is specified for the COM-radio audio runs?
practice.explanation: Shielded audio cable is the industry standard for avionics audio circuits.

preassessment.stem: Which wire type is used for COM-radio audio signals?
preassessment.explanation: Shielded audio cable is used for audio signals.

questions.learningObjective: Apply the staggered-splice rule when multiple splices are required in a bundle.
questions.fullObjective: Given a wire bundle that requires multiple in-line splices, apply the correct positioning rule to prevent bulges and mechanical stress.
questions.targetSourceStem: A wire harness requires multiple splices due to wire length limitations. How should the splice locations be arranged within the bundle?

flashcard.front: How should multiple in-bundle splices be positioned along a harness?
flashcard.back: Staggered along the run so no two splices line up at the same cross-section.
flashcard.explanation: Staggered splices avoid a thick hump in the bundle, keep clamping and sleeving easier, and reduce chafe risk. Grouping or centering splices creates stress concentrations.

jeopardy.clue: This is how multiple splices should be arranged along a bundle to prevent bulging.
choices: Located adjacent to the nearest connector
Centered on each individual wire run
jeopardy.explanation: Staggered placement keeps the bundle diameter uniform and prevents chafe and clamp issues.

multipleChoice.stem: A harness requires multiple splices. How should the splices be positioned within the bundle?
choices: Grouped together so they can be inspected easily
Placed adjacent to the nearest connector
Staggered along the run to prevent a bulge
multipleChoice.explanation: Staggering preserves bundle diameter and avoids stress concentrations.

practice.stem: Four splices are needed in a bundle that serves an overhead console. How should they be positioned?
choices: Grouped together in a single area
All placed next to the nearest connector
practice.explanation: Staggering distributes the bulk and keeps clamping and sleeving clean.

preassessment.stem: Where should splices be placed in a bundle with multiple splices?
preassessment.explanation: Splices must be staggered.

questions.learningObjective: State the maximum interval between wire identification markings.
questions.fullObjective: Given a wire marking requirement from AC 43.13-1B, state the maximum allowable distance between identification markings on a continuous wire run.
questions.targetSourceStem: According to AC 43.13-1B, what is the maximum distance between wire identification markings along a wire run?

flashcard.front: Per AC 43.13-1B, what is the maximum interval between wire identification markings?
flashcard.back: 15 inches between consecutive markings.
flashcard.explanation: AC 43.13-1B recommends markings at intervals not greater than 15 inches along a wire run, ensuring positive identification during maintenance.

jeopardy.clue: Per AC 43.13-1B, this is the maximum recommended interval between wire identification markings along a run.
choices: No interval is specified by the AC
jeopardy.explanation: AC 43.13-1B recommends markings no farther apart than 15 inches.

multipleChoice.stem: AC 43.13-1B addresses wire identification marking along a run. What maximum distance between markings is recommended?
multipleChoice.explanation: The AC recommends markings at intervals not greater than 15 inches.

practice.stem: A long wire run in an unpressurized area lacks intermediate markings. Per AC 43.13-1B, what is the maximum allowed interval?
practice.explanation: AC 43.13-1B recommends 15 inches as the maximum interval.

preassessment.stem: Maximum wire ID marking interval per AC 43.13-1B?
preassessment.explanation: 15 inches between markings.

questions.learningObjective: Select the correct connector type for a pressurized bulkhead penetration.
questions.fullObjective: Given a wire harness that must pass through a pressurized bulkhead, select the connector type that preserves cabin pressure at the penetration.
questions.targetSourceStem: A wire harness passes through a pressurized bulkhead. What type of connector maintains cabin pressure at the penetration?

flashcard.front: What type of connector maintains cabin pressure when a wire harness passes through a pressurized bulkhead?
flashcard.back: A pressure-seal feedthrough connector, often with hermetic seals around each contact.
flashcard.explanation: Ring terminals, butt splices, and D-sub connectors do not seal against pressure. A pressure-seal feedthrough is specifically rated for the pressure differential.

jeopardy.clue: This connector type maintains cabin pressure at the point a wire harness penetrates a pressure bulkhead.
choices: Ring terminal lug on a single stud
jeopardy.explanation: Pressure-seal feedthrough connectors are designed to carry the pressure differential at a bulkhead penetration.

multipleChoice.stem: Which connector type is installed where a wire harness passes through a pressurized bulkhead?
multipleChoice.explanation: Pressure-seal feedthrough connectors maintain cabin pressure at the bulkhead.

practice.stem: A bundle must pass through a pressurized fuselage bulkhead. Which connector preserves cabin pressure?
practice.explanation: Pressure-seal feedthroughs carry the pressure differential.

preassessment.stem: Which connector maintains pressure at a pressurized bulkhead penetration?
preassessment.explanation: A pressure-seal feedthrough is required where wiring passes through a pressurized bulkhead.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the harness-mounted EEPROM device storing aircraft configuration data.
questions.fullObjective: Given a description of aircraft configuration data stored in an EEPROM device integrated into the wiring harness, identify the industry name for this device.
questions.targetSourceStem: Aircraft configuration data is stored in an EEPROM device in the wiring harness. What is this device called?

flashcard.front: What is the harness-mounted EEPROM device that stores aircraft configuration data called?
flashcard.back: A configuration module.
flashcard.explanation: The configuration module holds aircraft-unique data so LRUs can be swapped without reprogramming. It is a passive EEPROM in the harness, often near an LRU connector.

jeopardy.clue: This harness-mounted EEPROM device stores aircraft-specific configuration data.
jeopardy.explanation: The configuration module is an EEPROM in the harness holding aircraft configuration data.

multipleChoice.stem: An EEPROM that stores aircraft configuration data resides in the wiring harness itself. What is this device called?
multipleChoice.explanation: Configuration modules keep aircraft data with the aircraft so LRUs can be swapped quickly.

practice.stem: When a pilot replaces an LRU, aircraft-specific data is preserved by an EEPROM in the wiring harness. What is this device called?
practice.explanation: It is the configuration module, holding aircraft-specific configuration data.

preassessment.stem: What is the EEPROM in a harness that stores aircraft configuration data called?
preassessment.explanation: Configuration module.

questions.learningObjective: Identify a typical SWAMP area on an aircraft.
questions.fullObjective: Given a list of aircraft locations, identify the one classified as a SWAMP area requiring special wire and harness protection against moisture and contaminants.
questions.targetSourceStem: Which aircraft location is designated as a SWAMP area requiring special wire protection?

flashcard.front: Which aircraft location is classified as a SWAMP area requiring special wire protection?
flashcard.back: The main wheel well area, due to water, mud, hydraulic fluid, and de-ice residue.
flashcard.explanation: SWAMP areas are Severely exposed to Water, chemicals, and Mechanical damage. Wheel wells and bilges qualify; pressurized avionics bays do not.

jeopardy.clue: This aircraft location is classified as a SWAMP area requiring special wire and harness protection.
choices: Instrument panel interior behind glare shield
jeopardy.explanation: Wheel wells are classic SWAMP areas: wet, chemical-exposed, and mechanically abused.

multipleChoice.stem: Which of the following is classified as a SWAMP area for wire harness protection?
choices: The interior of the instrument panel
multipleChoice.explanation: Wheel wells are Severely exposed to Water And Mechanical damage, qualifying as SWAMP areas.

practice.stem: A technician is specifying harness protection for several aircraft locations. Which location is a SWAMP area?
choices: The back of the instrument panel
The cabin ceiling space above the headliner
practice.explanation: The wheel well is a SWAMP location with water, chemicals, and debris exposure.

preassessment.stem: Which area is a SWAMP area?
preassessment.explanation: The main wheel well is a SWAMP area.

questions.learningObjective: Select a wiring technique that minimizes magnetic interference near a magnetometer.
questions.fullObjective: Given a magnetometer installation near other aircraft harnesses, select the wiring technique that best minimizes magnetic interference coupling into the sensor.
questions.targetSourceStem: What wiring technique minimizes magnetic interference near a magnetometer installation?

flashcard.front: Which wiring technique minimizes magnetic interference near a magnetometer?
flashcard.back: Configure the sensor and power wires as shielded twisted pairs.
flashcard.explanation: Twisted pairs cancel magnetic fields; shielding adds electric-field rejection. Rigid conduit can introduce ferrous distortion, and same-gauge rules do not address the issue.

jeopardy.clue: This wiring technique minimizes magnetic interference near a magnetometer installation.
choices: Route the wires through rigid metal conduit
Use the same gauge for every wire in the area
Connect every shield at the magnetometer end
jeopardy.explanation: Shielded twisted pairs cancel magnetic field coupling while still rejecting electric fields.

multipleChoice.stem: Which wiring technique best minimizes magnetic interference around a magnetometer installation?
choices: Route wires through rigid metal conduit
Use the same wire gauge for every circuit
Connect all shields at the magnetometer end
multipleChoice.explanation: Twisted pairs cancel the magnetic field created by balanced currents; shielding handles electric coupling.

practice.stem: A magnetometer mounted in a wingtip is picking up noise. What wiring technique should be applied in the area?
choices: Route all wires through rigid metal conduit
Use the same gauge for every circuit
Connect every shield at the magnetometer
practice.explanation: Shielded twisted pairs cancel magnetic interference and shield electric fields.

preassessment.stem: What wiring technique minimizes magnetic interference at a magnetometer?
preassessment.explanation: Shielded twisted pairs cancel magnetic fields.

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose a no-voltage condition at an LRU connector when the breaker output is correct.
questions.fullObjective: Given a circuit with voltage present at the breaker output but not at the LRU connector, identify the most likely installation error.
questions.targetSourceStem: After harness installation, no voltage is present at the LRU connector with the circuit breaker engaged. Voltage is correct at the breaker output. What is the likely error?

flashcard.front: Voltage is correct at the breaker output but missing at the LRU connector. What is the most likely installation error?
flashcard.back: A connector pin that is not seated, or the wire inserted into the wrong cavity.
flashcard.explanation: Pin-seating and wrong-cavity errors are the most common harness fabrication issues that break continuity when breaker-side voltage is correct.

jeopardy.clue: This installation error best explains correct voltage at the breaker but none at the LRU connector.
choices: Wire gauge too small for the circuit
Connector pin not seated or wrong cavity
Circuit breaker has high contact resistance
An internal short in the LRU pulls the line low
jeopardy.explanation: Seating/cavity errors break continuity and are the first thing to check when breaker-side power is good.

multipleChoice.stem: A technician measures correct voltage at the breaker output but zero at the LRU connector pin. What is the most likely installation error?
choices: The wire gauge is too small for the circuit
The connector pin is not seated or is in the wrong cavity
The circuit breaker has high contact resistance
An internal LRU short is pulling the line low
multipleChoice.explanation: Pin-seating and cavity errors break continuity and should be checked first.

practice.stem: Voltage is good at the breaker output but missing at the LRU connector pin. What should the technician suspect first?
choices: A wire gauge too small for the load
A pin not seated or placed in the wrong cavity
A circuit breaker with contact resistance
An internal short in the LRU
practice.explanation: Seating and cavity errors are the most common fabrication mistakes for this symptom.

preassessment.stem: Voltage is correct at the breaker but missing at the LRU connector. What is the likely error?
choices: Pin not seated or wrong cavity
preassessment.explanation: Seating/cavity error is most likely.

questions.learningObjective: Explain the primary reason for using stranded wire in aircraft power circuits.
questions.fullObjective: Given the choice between stranded and solid conductors of the same gauge, identify the primary reason stranded is specified in aircraft installations.
questions.targetSourceStem: Aircraft power wires use stranded construction rather than solid conductors of the same gauge. What is the primary reason for using stranded wire?

flashcard.front: Why is stranded wire specified for aircraft power circuits instead of solid wire?
flashcard.back: Stranded construction provides flexibility for routing and tolerates aircraft vibration without fatigue failures.
flashcard.explanation: At the same AWG, stranded and solid have similar DC resistance and ampacity, but stranded survives flex and vibration. Solid wire work-hardens and fails.

jeopardy.clue: This is the primary reason aircraft power wires are stranded rather than solid conductors of the same gauge.
choices: Higher current carrying capacity than solid
jeopardy.explanation: Stranded wire tolerates aircraft flex and vibration without the fatigue failures common in solid wire.

multipleChoice.stem: Why do aircraft power circuits use stranded wire rather than solid wire of the same gauge?
choices: To provide better electromagnetic shielding than solid
To lower overall electrical resistance compared with solid
To provide flexibility for routing and vibration tolerance
To provide higher current carrying capacity than solid
multipleChoice.explanation: Stranded construction survives the flexing and vibration encountered in aircraft.

practice.stem: A technician is asked why aircraft power wires are stranded. What is the primary reason?
choices: Better electromagnetic shielding than solid wire
Lower overall resistance than solid wire
Flexibility for routing and vibration tolerance
Greater current capacity than solid wire
practice.explanation: Stranded wire flexes without fatigue, which solid wire cannot do in aircraft service.

preassessment.stem: Why is stranded wire used in aircraft power circuits?
preassessment.explanation: Stranded wire provides the flexibility and vibration tolerance required in airframe installations.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the critical surface preparation step before installing a bonding jumper.
questions.fullObjective: Given a bonding-jumper installation to aircraft structure, identify the surface preparation step that is most critical to achieve low resistance.
questions.targetSourceStem: What is the most important surface preparation step before installing a bonding jumper to aircraft structure?

flashcard.front: What is the most important surface preparation step before installing a bonding jumper to structure?
flashcard.back: Remove all paint and primer down to bare metal at the contact area.
flashcard.explanation: Paint and primer are insulators. Bonding works only on bright, bare metal. Grease, roughening, and hole-drilling do not replace paint removal.

jeopardy.clue: This is the most critical surface preparation step before installing a bonding jumper to aircraft structure.
choices: Drill the bonding hole to exact tolerance
Apply a thick layer of conductive grease
Roughen the surface with a steel wire brush
jeopardy.explanation: Paint and primer are insulators; the joint must be bright, bare metal for low bonding resistance.

multipleChoice.stem: Before installing a bonding jumper to aircraft structure, which surface preparation step is most critical?
choices: Apply a thick coat of conductive grease to the area
Roughen the surface with a steel wire brush
Drill the bonding hole to an exact tolerance
Remove all paint and primer at the contact area
multipleChoice.explanation: The bonding surface must be bright, bare metal free of paint and primer.

practice.stem: A technician is preparing an airframe bonding location. What is the most important preparation step?
choices: Drill the hole to an exact tolerance
Apply a thick layer of conductive grease
Roughen the surface with a steel brush
Remove all paint and primer to bare metal
practice.explanation: Paint and primer must be fully removed to reach bare metal.

preassessment.stem: What is the most important surface prep step for a bonding jumper?
preassessment.explanation: Paint and primer removal is essential.

questions.learningObjective: Interpret the downward triangle with horizontal line on aircraft wiring diagrams.
questions.fullObjective: Given a wiring diagram symbol consisting of a downward-pointing triangle with a horizontal line, identify the type of electrical connection the symbol represents.
questions.targetSourceStem: On an aircraft wiring diagram, a triangle pointing down with a horizontal line represents what type of electrical connection?

flashcard.front: On an aircraft wiring diagram, what does a downward triangle with a horizontal line represent?
flashcard.back: An airframe structure ground point.
flashcard.explanation: The triangle-and-bar symbol is the airframe chassis ground. Signal references and battery negative points use distinct symbols.

jeopardy.clue: On an aircraft wiring diagram this downward-triangle-with-bar symbol represents this electrical connection.
jeopardy.explanation: The triangle-and-bar indicates airframe chassis ground.

multipleChoice.stem: On an aircraft wiring diagram, a downward-pointing triangle with a horizontal line represents which type of connection?
multipleChoice.explanation: That symbol denotes airframe chassis ground, distinct from signal reference or battery negative.

practice.stem: A technician reading a wiring diagram sees a downward triangle with a horizontal line. What connection does this represent?
practice.explanation: It is the airframe structure ground symbol.

preassessment.stem: What does a downward triangle with horizontal line represent on a wiring diagram?
preassessment.explanation: Airframe structure ground.

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose an 'ADS-B FAIL' message when the transponder replies to interrogation.
questions.fullObjective: Given an ADS-B FAIL annunciation after engine start and a transponder that still replies to ground interrogation, identify the most likely root cause.
questions.targetSourceStem: An aircraft equipped with ADS-B Out shows 'ADS-B FAIL' after engine start. The transponder replies normally to ground interrogation. What is the most likely cause?

flashcard.front: ADS-B FAIL is shown but the transponder still replies to ground interrogation. What is the most likely cause?
flashcard.back: The GPS position source is invalid or not being received by the transponder.
flashcard.explanation: ADS-B Out requires a valid position source meeting NIC/NAC. A Mode S transponder can still reply to interrogation without GPS, which explains the asymmetry.

jeopardy.clue: This is the most likely cause when ADS-B FAIL appears but the Mode S transponder still replies to ground interrogation.
choices: Mode S transponder code has not been set
Transponder antenna has high SWR on the run
GPS position source is invalid or not received
TCAS processor has failed its self-test
jeopardy.explanation: ADS-B Out needs valid GPS position; without it, the transponder still works but ADS-B fails.

multipleChoice.stem: ADS-B FAIL appears after engine start while the Mode S transponder continues to answer interrogations. What is the most likely root cause?
choices: The Mode S transponder code has not been set
The transponder antenna is showing high SWR
The GPS position source is invalid or not being received
The TCAS processor failed its self-test
multipleChoice.explanation: ADS-B Out depends on valid GPS; when GPS drops, ADS-B fails while the transponder still replies.

practice.stem: The crew taxis out and sees ADS-B FAIL, yet the transponder is replying normally. What should the technician check first?
choices: Mode S transponder squawk code entry
Transponder antenna SWR and coax integrity
GPS position source validity into the transponder
practice.explanation: ADS-B failing while the transponder replies points at the GPS position source.

preassessment.stem: ADS-B FAIL is displayed but the transponder replies normally. Most likely cause?
preassessment.explanation: GPS position source is invalid.

questions.learningObjective: Explain why maximum cruise speed is configured into a TCAS-equipped transponder.
questions.fullObjective: Given a TCAS installation, explain the role of the configured maximum cruise speed in collision avoidance advisories.
questions.targetSourceStem: During TCAS installation, the technician must configure the transponder for the aircraft's maximum cruise speed. Why is this parameter critical?

flashcard.front: Why must the aircraft's maximum cruise speed be configured during a TCAS installation?
flashcard.back: It sets the collision avoidance resolution advisory thresholds for that aircraft's closing rates.
flashcard.explanation: TCAS scales tau-based thresholds to aircraft performance. Wrong max speed skews RA timing, not reply timing or squawk code assignment.

jeopardy.clue: This TCAS behavior depends directly on the maximum cruise speed configured during installation.
jeopardy.explanation: TCAS uses configured max speed to scale RA thresholds appropriately.

multipleChoice.stem: Why must a technician configure a TCAS installation with the aircraft's maximum cruise speed?
choices: It establishes the ADS-B position update rate
It determines the reply delay for interrogations
It configures the squawk code assignment range
It sets the resolution advisory thresholds
multipleChoice.explanation: Max speed scales the tau-based RA thresholds TCAS uses.

practice.stem: When setting up a TCAS, the technician enters the aircraft's max cruise speed. What does this parameter govern?
practice.explanation: Max speed drives RA threshold scaling.

preassessment.stem: Why is max cruise speed configured during TCAS installation?
preassessment.explanation: It sets RA thresholds.

questions.learningObjective: Diagnose loss of TCAS targets at close range when the transponder tests normal.
questions.fullObjective: Given a TCAS display that loses targets at close range while the transponder tests normally, identify the installation element to investigate.
questions.targetSourceStem: A pilot reports TCAS shows traffic but the targets disappear when the other aircraft gets within 5 miles. The transponder tests normal. What should the technician investigate?

flashcard.front: TCAS targets disappear inside 5 miles while the transponder tests normal. What should be investigated?
flashcard.back: The TCAS directional antenna pattern and its installation location on the airframe.
flashcard.explanation: Targets dropping out at close range typically indicate a null or shadowed region in the directional antenna pattern, often due to poor mounting or nearby structures.

jeopardy.clue: This is the first thing to investigate when TCAS loses targets inside 5 nautical miles while the transponder tests normal.
choices: TCAS antenna pattern and installation location
jeopardy.explanation: Close-in target loss points to antenna pattern or shadowing issues at the TCAS directional antenna.

multipleChoice.stem: TCAS shows traffic, then loses targets when they come inside about 5 miles, yet the transponder tests normal. What should the technician investigate?
choices: The TCAS antenna pattern and installation location
The ADS-B Out message format configuration
The Mode C altitude encoder calibration
multipleChoice.explanation: Antenna pattern nulls near the airframe are the usual cause of close-in dropouts.

practice.stem: Close-in TCAS targets vanish while the transponder tests normal. What is the most productive first check?
choices: TCAS directional antenna pattern and mounting
practice.explanation: The TCAS directional antenna pattern and its location are the most likely cause.

preassessment.stem: TCAS drops targets inside 5 miles, transponder tests normal. Investigate what?
preassessment.explanation: Antenna pattern and location.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the pilot's required action when ADS-B Out is inoperative in mandated airspace.
questions.fullObjective: Given an aircraft with inoperative ADS-B Out intending to operate in ADS-B mandated airspace, identify the regulatory requirement imposed on the pilot.
questions.targetSourceStem: What is the consequence of operating in ADS-B mandated airspace with ADS-B Out inoperative?

flashcard.front: What must a pilot do to operate in ADS-B mandated airspace with inoperative ADS-B Out?
flashcard.back: Request an ATC deviation authorization in advance and operate as authorized.
flashcard.explanation: ATC deviation requests are required for non-equipped or inoperative ADS-B Out operations in mandated airspace. Squawking 7600 is for lost communication, not ADS-B.

jeopardy.clue: This is required of the pilot to operate in ADS-B mandated airspace with ADS-B Out inoperative.
choices: Rely on TCAS for automatic separation
Revert the transponder to Mode A only
jeopardy.explanation: Pilots must request an ATC deviation for ADS-B inoperative operations in mandated airspace.

multipleChoice.stem: An ADS-B Out system is inoperative and the pilot needs to operate in ADS-B mandated airspace. What must the pilot do?
choices: Squawk 7600 for the duration of the flight
Request a deviation authorization from ATC
Rely on TCAS to provide separation automatically
Switch the transponder to Mode A only
multipleChoice.explanation: Pilots must coordinate a deviation from ATC before operating ADS-B inoperative in mandated airspace.

practice.stem: With ADS-B Out inoperative, a pilot intends to enter ADS-B mandated airspace. What regulatory action is required?
practice.explanation: A deviation from ATC is required.

preassessment.stem: With ADS-B Out inoperative, the pilot must do what to enter mandated airspace?
preassessment.explanation: Request a deviation from ATC when the equipment required by the airspace is inoperative.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the ADS-B parameter to verify when position accuracy fails 91.227.
questions.fullObjective: Given a GPS receiver replacement that causes ADS-B Out position accuracy to fail 14 CFR 91.227, identify the ADS-B parameter the technician must verify.
questions.targetSourceStem: After a GPS receiver replacement, ADS-B Out position accuracy fails the 14 CFR 91.227 requirement. What parameter must the technician verify?

flashcard.front: Which ADS-B parameter must be verified when a GPS replacement fails 14 CFR 91.227 position accuracy?
flashcard.back: The NACp (Navigation Accuracy Category for position) value reported by ADS-B Out.
flashcard.explanation: 91.227 sets NACp, NIC, SIL, and SDA minimums. NACp is the metric for position accuracy and must be verified after a position-source change.

jeopardy.clue: This ADS-B parameter, required by 14 CFR 91.227, captures position accuracy and must be verified after a GPS swap.
jeopardy.explanation: NACp is the 91.227 position accuracy metric and must be verified after a GPS change.

multipleChoice.stem: A GPS receiver replacement causes ADS-B Out to fail the 14 CFR 91.227 position accuracy requirement. Which parameter must the technician verify?
multipleChoice.explanation: NACp (Navigation Accuracy Category for position) is the ADS-B position accuracy metric called out in 14 CFR 91.227.

practice.stem: After a GPS swap, an ADS-B Out test set shows a position-accuracy failure per 91.227. Which parameter does the technician verify?
practice.explanation: 91.227 points at NACp for position accuracy.

preassessment.stem: Which ADS-B parameter does 91.227 reference for position accuracy?
preassessment.explanation: NACp is the position accuracy metric.

questions.learningObjective: State the pilot action when a TCAS II RA commands CLIMB at service ceiling.
questions.fullObjective: Given a TCAS II CLIMB Resolution Advisory when the aircraft is already at its service ceiling, identify the pilot's required action per TCAS procedures.
questions.targetSourceStem: A TCAS II system displays a Resolution Advisory commanding 'CLIMB' but the aircraft is already at its service ceiling. What should the pilot do?

flashcard.front: What is the pilot action when a TCAS II CLIMB RA is issued at the aircraft's service ceiling?
flashcard.back: Adjust the RA response to level flight if the aircraft cannot safely climb beyond service ceiling.
flashcard.explanation: TCAS procedures accept a reduced response when performance prevents compliance. Ignoring the RA or disconnecting TCAS is not correct.

jeopardy.clue: This is the required response when a TCAS II CLIMB RA is issued at the aircraft's service ceiling.
choices: Follow the RA and exceed service ceiling
Ignore the RA and maintain altitude
Adjust the RA response to level flight
jeopardy.explanation: If performance cannot support a CLIMB, the pilot adjusts to a level-off response.

multipleChoice.stem: A TCAS II CLIMB RA appears, but the aircraft is already at service ceiling. What is the correct pilot response?
choices: Follow the RA and exceed service ceiling
Ignore the RA and maintain altitude
Adjust the RA response to level flight
multipleChoice.explanation: When the aircraft cannot safely climb, the pilot adjusts the response to level flight per procedure.

practice.stem: At service ceiling, the TCAS II issues a CLIMB RA. What response is correct?
choices: Ignore the RA and maintain altitude
practice.explanation: Level off when climb performance is not available.

preassessment.stem: Pilot response to a CLIMB RA at service ceiling?
preassessment.explanation: Follow the RA by adjusting the response to level flight; never ignore an RA.

questions.learningObjective: Isolate an ADS-B altitude error of 300 feet when an ADC supplies the transponder.
questions.fullObjective: Given an ADS-B test-set report of a 300-ft altitude error while the transponder uses the air data computer, identify the subsystems that must be checked.
questions.targetSourceStem: During ADS-B ground test, the test set shows correct position but altitude is reading 300 feet high. The transponder altitude source is the air data computer. What should be checked?

flashcard.front: ADS-B shows correct position but altitude 300 ft high with an ADC altitude source. What should be checked?
flashcard.back: The ADC static source integrity and the encoding altimeter calibration feeding the transponder.
flashcard.explanation: Transponder altitude errors with good position isolate to the static system and altitude encoder, not the transmitter, GPS antenna, or TCAS orientation.

jeopardy.clue: This pair of items should be checked when ADS-B altitude reads 300 ft high but position is correct.
choices: ADC static source and encoding altimeter calibration
jeopardy.explanation: Altitude errors with good position isolate to static-system and altitude-encoder calibration.

multipleChoice.stem: ADS-B position is correct but altitude reads 300 feet high, with an ADC as the altitude source. What should be checked?
choices: The ADC static source and encoding altimeter calibration
The GPS receiver antenna installation height
multipleChoice.explanation: Altitude errors point at static-source integrity and altitude-encoder calibration.

practice.stem: ADS-B position is correct but altitude is 300 ft high with an ADC feeding the transponder. What does the technician check?
choices: ADC static source and altitude encoding calibration
GPS antenna installation height above ground
practice.explanation: Static-system and altitude-encoder calibration are the targets.

preassessment.stem: ADS-B altitude 300 ft high, position correct, ADC source. Check what?
preassessment.explanation: ADC static source and encoding.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the TCAS traffic source for bearing and range.
questions.fullObjective: Given a TCAS traffic display, identify the signal source that provides bearing and range to the intruder aircraft.
questions.targetSourceStem: What traffic source provides TCAS with bearing and range to intruder aircraft?

flashcard.front: What traffic source provides TCAS with bearing and range to intruder aircraft?
flashcard.back: Active transponder interrogation and the reply timing and direction received by the TCAS processor.
flashcard.explanation: TCAS interrogates nearby transponders and uses reply timing for range and directional antenna data for bearing. ADS-B In and ground radar are supplemental, not TCAS core.

jeopardy.clue: This is the primary source TCAS uses to determine bearing and range to intruder aircraft.
jeopardy.explanation: TCAS uses its own Mode S/C interrogations and reply timing to derive bearing and range.

multipleChoice.stem: Which source gives a TCAS system the bearing and range to intruder aircraft?
multipleChoice.explanation: TCAS is an active-interrogation system; replies to its transmissions give range and bearing.

practice.stem: A TCAS II display shows bearing and range to traffic. Which source provides this data?
choices: Its own transponder interrogation and reply timing
practice.explanation: TCAS interrogates other transponders and uses reply timing and antenna direction.

preassessment.stem: What provides TCAS with bearing and range to traffic?
preassessment.explanation: Secondary surveillance uses interrogation and reply timing between the ground sensor and the transponder.

questions.learningObjective: Identify the document required to return an aircraft to service after pitot-static certification.
questions.fullObjective: Given a completed pitot-static certification test, identify the aircraft document that must be completed to return the aircraft to service.
questions.targetSourceStem: After completing a pitot-static system certification test, which document must the technician complete to return the aircraft to service?

flashcard.front: Which document returns an aircraft to service after pitot-static certification?
flashcard.back: An aircraft logbook entry containing the test data and the RTS approval signature.
flashcard.explanation: Pitot-static certification is not a major alteration; it is returned to service with a maintenance logbook entry per Part 43, not Form 337.

jeopardy.clue: After a pitot-static certification, this document returns the aircraft to service.
choices: Aircraft logbook entry with test data and approval
jeopardy.explanation: Pitot-static certification is documented in the aircraft logbook with the test data and approval for return to service.

multipleChoice.stem: After a pitot-static system certification test, which document must the technician complete to return the aircraft to service?
choices: An FAA Form 337 for major alteration
A logbook entry with test data and approval
multipleChoice.explanation: Part 43 requires a maintenance logbook entry with the test data and approval-for-return-to-service signature after pitot-static certification.

practice.stem: A technician finishes a 91.411 certification. What must be completed to return the aircraft to service?
choices: FAA Form 337 for a major alteration
Logbook entry with test data and approval
practice.explanation: A maintenance logbook entry containing the test data and an approval-for-return-to-service signature is required.

preassessment.stem: Which document returns an aircraft to service after pitot-static certification?
preassessment.explanation: Logbook entry.

questions.learningObjective: Identify documentation required for identical-part-number avionics replacement.
questions.fullObjective: Given an identical VHF COM radio replacement, identify the documentation required to return the aircraft to service.
questions.targetSourceStem: A technician replaces a VHF COM radio with an identical part number unit. What documentation is required?

flashcard.front: What documentation is required when a VHF COM radio is replaced with an identical part number unit?
flashcard.back: A maintenance logbook entry for the work performed under Part 43.
flashcard.explanation: Identical part-number replacement is maintenance, not a major alteration; Form 337 is not required. STC holder approval and FSDO sign-off are not applicable.

jeopardy.clue: This document is required when a VHF COM radio is swapped for an identical part number unit.
choices: STC approval from the radio manufacturer
An FAA Form 337 for major alteration
jeopardy.explanation: Identical part-number replacement is documented by a maintenance logbook entry.

multipleChoice.stem: A technician swaps a VHF COM radio with a unit bearing the identical part number. What documentation is required?
choices: An STC approval from the radio manufacturer
FSDO approval before return to flight
An FAA Form 337 for major alteration
A maintenance logbook entry for the work
multipleChoice.explanation: An identical swap is maintenance; only a logbook entry is required.

practice.stem: An identical VHF COM is installed to replace a failed unit. What documentation is required?
choices: An STC approval from the radio manufacturer
FSDO approval before return to flight
An FAA Form 337 for major alteration
A logbook entry documenting the maintenance
practice.explanation: A maintenance logbook entry is the required documentation.

preassessment.stem: What documentation is required for an identical-PN VHF COM swap?
preassessment.explanation: A maintenance logbook entry returns the aircraft to service after the work.

questions.learningObjective: Identify when an FAA Form 337 is required for avionics work.
questions.fullObjective: Given avionics maintenance scenarios, identify the condition that triggers the requirement to complete an FAA Form 337.
questions.targetSourceStem: When must an FAA Form 337 be completed for avionics work?

flashcard.front: When must an FAA Form 337 be completed for avionics work?
flashcard.back: When the work constitutes a major alteration to aircraft type design.
flashcard.explanation: Form 337 is required for major alterations and major repairs. Radio swaps, Part 145 maintenance, and component repairs do not automatically require a 337.

jeopardy.clue: This is the condition that requires an FAA Form 337 for avionics work.
choices: Major alterations to aircraft type design
Any radio replacement regardless of type
Any component repair returned to service
jeopardy.explanation: Form 337 is required for major alterations (and major repairs) to type design.

multipleChoice.stem: Under which condition must avionics work be documented on an FAA Form 337?
choices: When it is a major alteration to aircraft type design
When any radio is replaced, regardless of type
When any work is performed under a Part 145 certificate
When any component is repaired and returned to service
multipleChoice.explanation: Form 337 is triggered by major alterations or repairs to type design.

practice.stem: When is an FAA Form 337 required for an avionics installation?
choices: When the installation is a major alteration to type design
When any radio is replaced in the aircraft
For any component bench repair returned to service
practice.explanation: Major alteration to type design is the trigger.

preassessment.stem: When is Form 337 required?
preassessment.explanation: FAA Form 337 is required for major alterations to the aircraft's type design; not every repair or radio swap reaches that threshold.

questions.learningObjective: Identify who signs the logbook RTS after an STC autopilot installation.
questions.fullObjective: Given a completed STC autopilot installation where all paperwork is correct, identify who signs the aircraft logbook returning the aircraft to service.
questions.targetSourceStem: A technician completes installation of a new autopilot under an STC. The work order references the STC number and all documents are complete. Who signs the aircraft logbook returning it to service?

flashcard.front: Who signs the logbook returning the aircraft to service after an STC autopilot installation?
flashcard.back: The technician who performed the work, with proper authorization (A&P or Part 145 certifying staff).
flashcard.explanation: Under Part 43, the certifying technician signs the RTS. An IA is not required simply because an STC was used; major alterations do need IA sign-off on Form 337, not the logbook RTS.

jeopardy.clue: This person signs the aircraft logbook returning an aircraft to service after completing an STC autopilot installation.
choices: The technician who performed the work
Only the IA who supervised the work
jeopardy.explanation: The certifying technician (A&P or repair station authorized person) signs the logbook RTS.

multipleChoice.stem: After installing a new autopilot under an STC, who signs the aircraft logbook returning the aircraft to service?
choices: Only the IA who supervised the installation
The technician who performed the work
multipleChoice.explanation: The certifying technician signs the logbook RTS; an IA signature on Form 337 covers the major alteration itself.

practice.stem: An STC autopilot installation is complete and documentation is in order. Who signs the RTS in the aircraft logbook?
choices: A representative of the STC holder
The technician who performed the work
Only the IA who supervised the work
practice.explanation: The certifying technician who performed the work signs the return-to-service entry.

preassessment.stem: Who signs RTS after an STC autopilot install?
preassessment.explanation: Certifying technician.

questions.learningObjective: Document troubleshooting activity that replaced multiple LRUs.
questions.fullObjective: Given a troubleshooting effort that replaced three LRUs before finding the fault, identify the correct documentation practice in the work order.
questions.targetSourceStem: During troubleshooting, a technician replaces three LRUs before finding the fault. How should this be documented in the work order?

flashcard.front: A technician replaced three LRUs before finding the real fault. How should this be documented in the work order?
flashcard.back: List all replaced components and the troubleshooting steps taken during the process.
flashcard.explanation: Complete documentation supports traceability, warranty, and future troubleshooting. Hiding interim replacements is not acceptable.

jeopardy.clue: This is the correct work-order approach when several LRUs were replaced during troubleshooting before the real fault was found.
choices: Document only the final repair that fixed the problem
Note that warranty may be void for excessive swapping
List all components replaced and troubleshooting steps
Request engineering review before completing the documentation
jeopardy.explanation: Every replacement and step must be captured for traceability and future work.

multipleChoice.stem: A technician swapped three LRUs before isolating the fault. How should this be documented on the work order?
choices: Note that warranty may be void due to excessive swapping
Document only the final repair that resolved the issue
List every component replaced and the troubleshooting steps taken
Request an engineering review before completing documentation
multipleChoice.explanation: All replacements and steps are documented for traceability and warranty.

practice.stem: Three LRUs were replaced before the real fault was found. How is the work order properly completed?
choices: Document only the final repair that fixed the issue
Note the warranty as void for excessive swapping
List all replaced components and the troubleshooting steps
Request an engineering review before documenting
practice.explanation: Document every replacement and every step taken.

preassessment.stem: How do you document troubleshooting that replaced three LRUs?
preassessment.explanation: List all replacements and steps.

questions.learningObjective: Verify AIRAC cycle currency before a navigation database load.
questions.fullObjective: Given an FMS navigation database update, identify the first verification the technician must perform before loading the database.
questions.targetSourceStem: Before loading a navigation database update into an FMS, what must the technician verify first?

flashcard.front: What must the technician verify first before loading a navigation database update into an FMS?
flashcard.back: That the database effective date matches the current AIRAC cycle for the intended service window.
flashcard.explanation: AIRAC effectivity is the threshold check. Flight recency, autopilot self-test, and display power-state are not prerequisites for a nav-database load.

jeopardy.clue: This is the first thing a technician verifies before loading an FMS navigation database update.
choices: Aircraft has flown within the last 30 days
All cockpit displays are currently powered on
Database effective date matches current AIRAC cycle
jeopardy.explanation: The effective date must match the current AIRAC cycle and cover the intended period of use.

multipleChoice.stem: Before loading an FMS navigation database update, what must the technician verify first?
choices: Every cockpit display is already powered on
The aircraft has flown within the last 30 days
The autopilot servo self-test has just passed
The database effective date matches the current AIRAC cycle
multipleChoice.explanation: AIRAC cycle currency is the first prerequisite for a nav-database load.

practice.stem: A technician is about to load a nav database. What is the first verification?
practice.explanation: Verify the database effective date matches the current AIRAC cycle before dispatch; a stale database fails IFR legality.

preassessment.stem: First verification before an FMS nav-database load?
preassessment.explanation: AIRAC effective date match.

questions.learningObjective: Recover from an avionics software load that fails at 80%.
questions.fullObjective: Given an avionics software load that fails at 80 percent and displays INVALID, identify the correct recovery action.
questions.targetSourceStem: A technician is updating avionics software using a data loader. The upload fails at 80% and the unit displays 'INVALID'. What is the correct recovery action?

flashcard.front: A software load fails at 80% with an INVALID message. What is the correct recovery action?
